blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ X
CHARACTERIZATION AND CLASSIFICATION OF GROUPS OF MULTIPLICATIVE TYPEby A. Grothendieck0

footnotes.html: xy version of November 6, 2009: Addenda placed in Sect. 9, revised up to 8.8

pages.title: 1. Classification of isotrivial groups. Case of a base field

blocks.html: 1. Classification of isotrivial groups. Case of a base field

Recall (IX 1.1) that the group of multiplicative type H over the prescheme S is said to be isotrivial if there exists a finite surjective étale morphism \(S' \to S\) such that \(H' = H \times_S S'\) is diagonalizable. When S is connected, then \(S'\) decomposes into a finite sum of connected components \(S'_i\), and one may therefore (after replacing \(S'\) by one of the \(S'_i\)) assume \(S'\) connected. Finally, one knows that one can dominate \(S'\) by an \(S'_1\) finite étale and connected, which is Galois, i.e. a principal homogeneous fibre bundle over S with group \(\Gamma_S\), where \(\Gamma\) is an ordinary finite group (cf. SGA 1, V Nos 7 and 3 when S is locally noetherian, and EGA IV4, 18.2.9 in the general case).1 We assume \(S'\) chosen in this way, and we propose to determine the groups of multiplicative type H over S that are "trivialized" by \(S'\), i.e. such that \(H' = H \times_S S'\) is diagonalizable.2 By descent theory (cf. SGA 1, VIII 5.4), the category of these H is equivalent to the category of diagonalizable groups \(H'\) over \(S'\), equipped with operations of \(\Gamma\) on \(H'\) compatible with the operations of \(\Gamma\) on \(S'\). (N. B. As the groups under consideration are affine over the base, the question of effectivity of a descent datum is answered in the affirmative, cf. SGA 1, VIII 2.1). Now, \(S'\) being connected, the contravariant functor \[ M \longmapsto \mathbf{D}_{S'}(M) \] is an anti-equivalence of the category of ordinary commutative groups with the category of diagonalizable groups over \(S'\) (cf. VIII 1.6), a quasi-inverse functor of which is \(H \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{S'\text{-gr.}}(H, \mathbb{G}_{m,S'})\).3

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1

Let S be a connected prescheme, \(S'\) a connected principal covering of S with group \(\Gamma\) (finite). Then the category of groups of multiplicative type over S split4 by \(S'\), is anti-equivalent to the category of \(\Gamma\)-modules, i.e. of ordinary commutative groups M equipped with a homomorphism \(\Gamma \to \mathrm{Aut}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M)\).
From this one concludes in the standard way:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.2

Let S be a connected prescheme, \(\xi : \mathrm{Spec}(\Omega) \to S\) a "geometric point" of S, i.e. a homomorphism into S of the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(\Omega\); consider the fundamental group of S at \(\xi\) (cf. SGA 1 V, N°7): \[ \pi = \pi_1(S,\xi). \] Then the category of isotrivial groups of multiplicative type H over S is anti-equivalent to the category of "Galois modules" under \(\pi\), i.e. of \(\pi\)-modules M such that the stabilizer in \(\pi\) of every point of M is an open subgroup.

In this correspondence, to the isotrivial group of multiplicative type H is associated the group \(M = \mathrm{Hom}_{\Omega\text{-gr.}}(H_\xi, \mathbb{G}_{m,\Omega})\), where \(H_\xi\) is the fibre of H at \(\xi\); this group comes naturally equipped with operations of \(\pi_1(S,\xi)\).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 1.3

We shall see below (cf. 5.16) that if S is normal, or more generally geometrically unibranch, then every group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S is necessarily isotrivial, so the classification principle 1.2 applies to groups of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, which correspond to the Galois \(\pi\)-modules that are of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\). For the moment, let us content ourselves with the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4

Let k be a field, H a group of multiplicative type and of finite type over k; then H is isotrivial, i.e. there exists a finite separable extension \(k'\) of k that splits H. Consequently, by virtue of 1.2, if \(\pi\) is the topological Galois group of an algebraic closure \(\Omega\) of k, the category of groups of multiplicative type and of finite type H over k is anti-equivalent to the category of Galois modules M under \(\pi\) that are of finite type as \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules.

It follows first from the fact that H is of finite type over k and from the "principle of the finite extension" (cf. EGA IV3, 9.1.4) that there exists a finite extension \(k'\) of k that splits H. Let us recall the principle of the proof: by hypothesis there exist a diagonalizable group of finite type G over k, an \(S'\) faithfully flat over \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\), and an isomorphism of \(S'\)-groups \(H_{S'} \simeq G_{S'}\). After replacing \(S'\) by the residue field of a point of \(S'\), we may assume that \(S'\) is the spectrum of an extension K of k. The latter is the inductive limit of its subalgebras \(A_i\) of finite type, from which it follows easily (cf. EGA IV3, 8.8.2.4) that u comes from an \(A_i\)-isomorphism \(u_i : H_{A_i} \simeq G_{A_i}\) for i large enough. By virtue of the Nullstellensatz, there exists a quotient ring \(k'\) of \(A_i\) which is a finite extension of k. The latter therefore splits H.

Then \(k'\) is a purely inseparable extension of a separable extension \(k'_s\) of k. By virtue of IX 5.4 the isomorphism \(u' : H_{k'} \simeq G_{k'}\) comes from an isomorphism \(H_{k'_s} \simeq G_{k'_s}\), which proves that \(k'_s\) splits H and establishes 1.4.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 1.5

The statement 1.2 provides in particular a characterization of the isotrivial tori over S of relative dimension n: setting \(\pi = \pi_1(S,\xi)\), they correspond to the classes (up to "equivalence") of representations \(\pi \to \mathrm{GL}(n,\mathbb{Z})\) whose kernel is an open subgroup of \(\pi\).

Even when S is an algebraic curve, there may exist over S tori (of relative dimension 2) that are not locally isotrivial (and a fortiori not isotrivial); there may also exist locally trivial tori that are not isotrivial. (Note however that such phenomena can occur only if S is not normal, as was already pointed out in 1.3). Let for example S be an irreducible algebraic curve (over an algebraically closed field, to fix ideas) having an ordinary double point a, let \(S'\) be the normalization, and b and c the two points of \(S'\) above a. One then constructs a principal homogeneous fibre bundle P over S, with structure group \(\mathbb{Z}\), connected, by attaching to one another infinitely many copies of \(S'\) according to the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \cdots & S'_{-1} \ar@{-}[dr]^-{b} & & S'_0 \ar@{-}[dl]_-{c} \ar@{-}[dr]^-{b} & & S'_1 \ar@{-}[dl]_-{c} \ar@{-}[dr]^-{b} & & \cdots \\ & & {\bullet} & & {\bullet} & & {\bullet} & } \] (N. B. This is a principal bundle in the sense of the étale topology). Now we have a homomorphism \[ \varphi : \mathbb{Z} \longrightarrow \mathrm{GL}(2,\mathbb{Z}), \qquad \varphi(n) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & n \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \] which makes it possible to construct a torus T over S, of relative dimension 2, from the trivial torus \(\mathbb{G}_m^2\) over P and the descent datum on the latter deduced from \(\varphi\). (N. B. one will note that the projection \(\mathrm{P} \to \mathrm{S}\) is covering for the étale topology and a fortiori for the canonical topology of \((\mathbf{Sch})\), and that the descent datum under consideration is necessarily effective, since \(\mathbb{G}^2_{m,\mathrm{P}}\) is affine over P). It is not difficult to prove that T is not isotrivial in a neighbourhood of a5 (it is however trivial in \(\mathrm{S} - \{a\}\)).

One finds a variant of this construction by taking for S a curve having two irreducible components \(\mathrm{S}_1\) and \(\mathrm{S}_2\) meeting in two points \(a'\) and \(a''\), which makes it possible to construct a principal homogeneous fibre bundle P over S with group \(\mathbb{Z}_S\), connected, and locally trivial, whence an associated torus T which is locally trivial, but not isotrivial.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: More precisely, this follows from the "axiomatic conditions for a Galois theory", cf. SGA 1, V No 4 g); when S is locally noetherian, the verification of the axioms is carried out in loc. cit., Nos 7 & 3, in particular 3.7, which relies on SGA 1, I 10.9. This last result is proved, without noetherian hypotheses, in EGA IV4, 18.2.9.
N.D.E.: In what follows, we shall say that an S-group of multiplicative type is "split" if it is "trivial" in the sense of IX 1.2, i.e., if it is a diagonalizable S-group.
N.D.E.: S has been corrected to \(\mathrm{S}'\).
N.D.E.: Here and in what follows, "splittés" has been replaced by "déployés" (split), "splitte" by "déploie" (splits), etc.
N.D.E.: See 7.3 below.

blocks.html: 2. Infinitesimal variations of structure6
Let us begin by recalling the following result (cf. SGA 1, I 8.3 in the case S locally noetherian, EGA IV4, 18.1.2 in general):

blocks.title: Rappel
blocks.html:
Reminder 2.0

Let S be a prescheme, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a subprescheme having the same underlying set. Then the functor \[ X \longmapsto X_0 = X \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}_0 \] is an equivalence between the category of preschemes étale over S and the analogous category over \(\mathrm{S}_0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

Let S be a prescheme, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a subprescheme having the same underlying set (i.e. defined by a nil-ideal \(\mathscr{I}\)). Then the functor \[ \mathrm{H} \longmapsto \mathrm{H}_0 = \mathrm{H} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}_0 \] from the category of preschemes in groups of multiplicative type over S to the analogous category over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), is fully faithful. Moreover, it induces an equivalence between the category of quasi-isotrivial groups of multiplicative type over S and the analogous category over \(\mathrm{S}_0\).

Let us first prove the full faithfulness, i.e. that if H, G over S are of multiplicative type, then \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{H},\mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{H}_0,\mathrm{G}_0) \] is bijective. The question being local on S, we may assume S affine; there then exists a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism \(S' \to S\) that splits H and G. Let \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\); denote by \(H', G'\) resp. \(H'', G''\) the groups deduced from H, G by the base change \(S' \to S\) resp. \(S'' \to S\), and define likewise \(S'_0\) and \(S''_0\), the latter being also isomorphic to \(S'_0 \times_{S_0} S'_0\). One then finds a commutative diagram with exact rows: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{u} & \mathrm{Hom}_{S'\text{-gr.}}(H',G') \ar[d]^-{u'} \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S''\text{-gr.}}(H'',G'') \ar[d]^-{u''} \\ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(H_0,G_0) \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S'_0\text{-gr.}}(H'_0,G'_0) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S''_0\text{-gr.}}(H''_0,G''_0) } \] so to prove that u is bijective, it suffices to prove that this is so for \(u'\) and \(u''\), which reduces us to the case where H and G are diagonalizable, hence of the form \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\) and \(\mathbf{D}_S(N)\), where M and N are ordinary commutative groups. We shall then likewise have \(H_0 = \mathbf{D}_{S_0}(M)\), \(G_0 = \mathbf{D}_{S_0}(N)\). We then have a commutative diagram7 \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(N_S,M_S) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathbf{D}_S(M),\mathbf{D}_S(N)) \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(N_{S_0},M_{S_0}) \ar[r]^-{\sim} & \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(\mathbf{D}_{S_0}(M),\mathbf{D}_{S_0}(N)) } \] where the horizontal arrows are isomorphisms by virtue of VIII 1.4, so we are reduced to proving that the homomorphism \[ \text{(x)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(N_S,M_S) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(N_{S_0},M_{S_0}) \] is bijective, i.e. to proving that the functor \(M_S \mapsto M_{S_0}\), from constant commutative group preschemes over S to constant commutative group preschemes over \(S_0\), is fully faithful. Now (x) is also identified with the natural map \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(N,\Gamma(M_S)) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(N,\Gamma(M_{S_0})) \] deduced from \(\Gamma(M_S) \to \Gamma(M_{S_0})\); but this last map is obviously bijective (for \(\Gamma(M_S)\) = the set of locally constant maps from S to M, depends only on the topological space underlying S), whence the desired conclusion.

To prove the second assertion of 2.1, it remains to see that every group of multiplicative type \(H_0\) over \(S_0\) which is quasi-isotrivial comes from a quasi-isotrivial group of multiplicative type H over S. To see this, let \(S'_0 \to S_0\) be a surjective étale morphism that splits \(H_0\). One knows (cf. 2.0) that there exist an étale morphism \(S' \to S\) and an \(S_0\)-isomorphism \(S' \times_S S_0 \simeq S'_0\), so we may assume that \(S'_0\) comes from \(S'\) by reduction. As \(H'_0\) is diagonalizable, one sees at once that it is isomorphic to the group deduced by the base change \(S'_0 \to S'\) from a diagonalizable group \(H'\) over \(S'\) (N. B. if \(H'_0 = \mathbf{D}_{S'_0}(M)\), one takes \(H' = \mathbf{D}_{S'}(M)\)). Set as usual \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\), \(S''' = S' \times_S S' \times_S S'\), and define likewise \(S''_0\), \(S'''_0\), deduced from the preceding by the base change \(S_0 \to S\) and also isomorphic to the fibred square resp. cube of \(S'_0\) over \(S_0\). Using the full faithfulness already proved, in the cases \((S'',S''_0)\) and \((S''',S'''_0)\), one sees that the natural descent datum on \(H'_0\) relative to \(S'_0 \to S_0\) (cf. IV 2.1) comes from a well-determined descent datum on \(H'\) relative to \(S' \to S\). This descent datum is effective since \(H'\) is affine over \(S'\) (SGA 1, VIII 2.1), so there exists an S-group H such that \(H \times_S S' = H' = \mathbf{D}_{S'}(M)\), and H is therefore of multiplicative type and quasi-isotrivial. One then verifies easily, using now the full faithfulness result for \((S',S'_0)\), that the given isomorphism between \(H'_0\) and \(H' \times_{S'} S'_0\) comes from an isomorphism between \(H_0\) and \(H \times_S S_0\). (For a more formal exposition of results of this type, see the article by Giraud in preparation(*) on descent theory).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

Let H be an S-group of multiplicative type, and \(H_0 = H \times_S S_0\). For H to be quasi-isotrivial (resp. locally isotrivial, resp. isotrivial, resp. locally trivial, resp. trivial), it is necessary and sufficient that \(H_0\) be so.

The "necessary" part is trivial; the "sufficient" part has already been seen in the quasi-isotrivial case, for thanks to full faithfulness, it suffices to know that every quasi-isotrivial group over \(S_0\) lifts to a quasi-isotrivial group over S. The same argument works for "trivial". For the "isotrivial" case, one repeats the reasoning establishing the second assertion of 2.1, but taking \(S'_0 \to S_0\) étale surjective and finite. The cases "locally isotrivial" and "locally trivial" follow at once from the cases "isotrivial" and "trivial".
One can generalize 2.2 a little when \(\mathscr{I}\) is nilpotent, by not assuming a priori that H is of multiplicative type:8

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Suppose the ideal \(\mathscr{I}\) defining \(S_0\) is locally nilpotent. Let H be a group prescheme over S, flat over S, and \(H_0 = H \times_S S_0\). For H to be of multiplicative type and quasi-isotrivial, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H_0\) be so.

Indeed, suppose \(H_0\) is of multiplicative type and quasi-isotrivial; let us prove that H is. The question being local for the étale topology, and the category of preschemes étale over S being equivalent to the category of preschemes étale over \(S_0\) via the functor \(S' \mapsto S' \times_S S_0\) (cf. 2.0), we are at once reduced to the case where \(H_0\) is diagonalizable, hence isomorphic to a group \(\mathbf{D}_{S_0}(M)\). Let \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\); we thus have an isomorphism \(u_0 : H_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } G_0\), and I claim that it comes from a unique homomorphism \(u : H \to G\), which will then be an isomorphism since \(u_0\) is (H and G being flat over S, and \(\mathscr{I}\) locally nilpotent9), which will establish 2.3. Now we have (cf. VIII 1 (xxx)) \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,\mathbb{G}_{m,S})), \] and the second member is also identified with \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,\mathbb{G}_{m,S})), \] so the homomorphism \[ \text{(x)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(H_0,G_0) \] is isomorphic to the homomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,\mathbb{G}_{m,S})) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(H_0,\mathbb{G}_{m,S_0})) \] deduced from the restriction homomorphism \[ \text{(xx)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,\mathbb{G}_{m,S}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(H_0,\mathbb{G}_{m,S_0}), \] so to prove that (x) is bijective, it suffices to prove that (xx) is. The question is local on S, so we may assume S affine. Now \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) being commutative and smooth over S, the situation falls under IX 3.6, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let A be an artinian local ring with residue field k, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\).

  1. (i)

    10 Let H be an S-group prescheme, flat and locally of finite type, such that \(H_0 = H \times_S S_0\) is of multiplicative type. Then H is of multiplicative type, of finite type and isotrivial. In particular, every S-group prescheme H of multiplicative type and of finite type is isotrivial.

  2. (ii)

    The functor \(H \mapsto H_0\) is an equivalence between the category of groups of multiplicative type of finite type over A and the analogous category over k.

Indeed, let H be as in (i). Then \(H_0\) is of multiplicative type and locally of finite type, hence of finite type, hence isotrivial by 1.4. Therefore, by 2.3 and 2.2, H is of multiplicative type (hence of finite type) and isotrivial. Assertion (ii) then follows from 2.1.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let \(S' \to S\) be a faithfully flat and radicial morphism.

  1. (i)

    The functor \(H \mapsto H' = H \times_S S'\), from the category of groups of multiplicative type over S to the analogous category over \(S'\), is fully faithful. Moreover, it induces an equivalence between the subcategories formed by the quasi-isotrivial groups of multiplicative type.

  2. (ii)

    If H is of multiplicative type, for it to be quasi-isotrivial (resp. locally isotrivial, resp. isotrivial, resp. locally trivial, resp. trivial) it is necessary and sufficient that \(H'\) be so.

Indeed, let \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) and \(S''' = S' \times_S S' \times_S S'\); then the hypothesis that \(S' \to S\) is radicial implies that the diagonal immersions \(S' \to S''\) and \(S' \to S'''\) are surjective, so base change by either of these immersions falls under 2.1 and 2.2. Taking into account that \(S' \to S\) is an effective descent morphism for the fibred category of groups of multiplicative type over preschemes (for it is faithfully flat and quasi-compact11), our assertion follows formally from 2.1 and 2.2 (cf. for a formal treatment the article by Giraud already cited).

N.D.E.: Recall that 2.3 is used to prove Theorem IX 3.6 bis.

N.D.E.: As \(u_0\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to see that for every \(h \in \mathrm{H}\), the morphism \(\varphi : \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G},u(h)} \to \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{H},h}\) is bijective. It is so upon reduction modulo \(\mathrm{I} = \mathrm{I}_s\) (where s is the image of h in S), so its cokernel C satisfies \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{IC}\), whence \(\mathrm{C} = 0\) since I is nilpotent. Then, as \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{H},h}\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}\), the kernel K of \(\varphi\) also satisfies \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{IK}\), whence \(\mathrm{K} = 0\).

pages.title: 3. Finite variations of structure: complete base ring

blocks.html: 3. Finite variations of structure: complete base ring

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

Let A be a noetherian ring, equipped with an ideal I such that A is separated and complete for the I-adic topology, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(A/I)\), G and H two S-group schemes, with G of multiplicative type and isotrivial, H affine over S, flat over S at the points of \(H_0 = H \times_S S_0\), and \(H_0\) of multiplicative type and quasi-isotrivial. Then the natural map \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(G_0,H_0) \] is bijective.

For every integer \(n \geqslant 0\), let \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(A/I^{n+1})\), and let \(G_n, H_n\) be the groups deduced from G, H by the base change \(S_n \to S\). As G/S is of multiplicative type and isotrivial and H is affine over S, then, by IX 7.1, the natural homomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \mathrm{Hom}_{S_n\text{-gr.}}(G_n,H_n) \] is bijective. On the other hand, by virtue of 2.3, the \(H_n\) are of multiplicative type and quasi-isotrivial, and by virtue of 2.1 the transition homomorphisms in the projective system \((\mathrm{Hom}_{S_n\text{-gr.}}(G_n,H_n))_n\) are isomorphisms, whence 3.1 at once.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2

Let A be a noetherian ring equipped with an ideal I such that A is separated and complete for the I-adic topology, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(A/I)\). Then:

  1. (i)

    The functor \(H \mapsto H_0 = H \times_S S_0\) is an equivalence between the category of isotrivial groups of multiplicative type over S and the analogous category over \(S_0\).

  2. (ii)

    Let H be an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type; for H to be isotrivial, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H_0\) be so.

Proof. For (i) one may either repeat the proof of 2.1, or use 1.2, using in either case the fact that the functor \(S' \mapsto S'_0 = S' \times_S S_0\) from the category of finite étale schemes over S to the category of finite étale schemes over \(S_0\) is an equivalence (SGA 1, I 8.4), which can also be stated (reducing to the case S connected, i.e. \(S_0\) connected), choosing a geometric point \(\xi\) of \(S_0\), by saying that the canonical homomorphism \[ \pi_1(S_0,\xi) \longrightarrow \pi_1(S,\xi) \] is an isomorphism.

Let us prove (ii), i.e. that if \(H_0\) is isotrivial, then H is. By virtue of (i), there exist an isotrivial group of multiplicative type G over S and an \(S_0\)-isomorphism \[ u_0 : G_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H_0. \] As H is of finite type, so are \(H_0\) and \(G_0\); therefore, by IX 2.1 b), the type of G at each point of S is an abelian group of finite type, and hence G is of finite type over S. On the other hand, by virtue of 3.1, \(u_0\) comes from a homomorphism of S-groups \[ u : G \longrightarrow H. \] Finally, as G, H are of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, and as \(u_0\) is an isomorphism, then, by IX 2.9, u is an isomorphism (taking into account that every neighbourhood of \(S_0\) in S is equal to S).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring with residue field k.

  1. (i)

    Every group of multiplicative type and of finite type over A is isotrivial.

  2. (ii)

    The functor \(H \mapsto H \otimes_A k = H_0\) is an equivalence between the categories of groups of multiplicative type and of finite type over A and over k.
12 First, (i) follows from 3.2 (ii) and 1.4; then (ii) follows from 3.2 (i), taking into account the fact that H is of finite type if and only if \(H_0\) is (cf. the proof of 3.2 (ii)).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 3.3.1

One will note that 3.3 gives, by virtue of 1.4, a complete classification of the groups of multiplicative type and of finite type over A in terms of the topological Galois group of an algebraic closure \(\Omega\) of k.

blocks.title: Remarques
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.4

Under the hypotheses of 3.2 (i.e. A noetherian, separated and complete for the I-adic topology, but with no other hypothesis on A/I), it will follow from N°5 that the functor \(H \mapsto H_0\), from the category of groups of multiplicative type and of finite type over S to the analogous category over \(S_0\), is still fully faithful (without isotriviality hypotheses).13 However, it is not in general essentially surjective; in fact there may exist an \(S_0\)-group \(H_0\), of multiplicative type and of finite type, locally trivial if one insists (but not isotrivial), which does not come by reduction from a group of multiplicative type H over S. To see this, let us take up one or the other of the examples 1.6 of a non-isotrivial group of multiplicative type over a non-normal curve. One may obviously take this curve affine, say \(S_0\), and assume it embedded in the affine space of dimension 2, hence defined by an ideal J in \(k[X,Y]\). We shall take for A the completion of this ring for the J-preadic topology, so that A is a regular ring of dimension 2, a fortiori normal. We shall see in 5.16 that it follows that every group of multiplicative type and of finite type over \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\) is isotrivial; therefore \(H_0\), which is of finite type and not isotrivial, does not come from a group of multiplicative type H over S (for H would necessarily be of finite type, hence isotrivial).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.5

(*)14 Let S be a prescheme, \(u : G \to H\) a homomorphism of S-group preschemes locally of finite presentation and flat over S, U the set of \(s \in S\) such that the homomorphism induced on the fibres \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is flat (resp. smooth, resp. unramified, resp. étale, resp. quasi-finite). Then U is open, and the restriction \(u|_U : G|_U \to H|_U\) is a flat (resp. smooth, resp. unramified, resp. étale, resp. quasi-finite) morphism.

Indeed, let V be the set of points at which u is flat (resp. …). One knows that V is open (cf. SGA 1, I to IV in the locally noetherian case, EGA IV in general), and that for \(x \in G\) above \(s \in S\), one has \(x \in V\) if and only if \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is flat (resp. …) at x (same reference; in the flat, smooth, étale cases, one uses here the flatness of G and H over S). As \(u_s\) is a homomorphism of locally algebraic groups, this also means that \(u_s\) is flat (resp. …) everywhere (Exp. VIB, 1.3), i.e. \(s \in U\). Therefore U is the inverse image of the open set V by the unit section of G, hence open, and \(V = G|_U\), so \(G|_U \to H|_U\) is flat (resp. …), which completes the proof. (N. B. in the "unramified" or "quasi-finite" case, the flatness hypothesis on G and H is unnecessary).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.6

Let H be a commutative algebraic group over a field k, admitting an open subgroup G of multiplicative type. Then the family of subschemes \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) (\(n > 0\)) of H is schematically dense; in particular if one has \({}_n\mathrm{H} = {}_n\mathrm{G}\) for all \(n > 0\), then \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{G}\).

Here \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) denotes the kernel of \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{H}}\).15 Let \(\overline{k}\) be an algebraic closure of k; it suffices to show that the family \(({}_n\mathrm{H}_{\overline{k}})_{n>0}\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{H}_{\overline{k}}\), for then the family \(({}_n\mathrm{H})_{n>0}\) will be so in H (cf. IX 4.5). Hence we may assume k algebraically closed, so G of the form \(\mathbf{D}_k(M)\), M an ordinary commutative group of finite type. Let \(M_0 = M/\mathrm{Torsion}(M)\); then \(T = \mathbf{D}_k(M_0)\) is the largest torus contained in G, and \(H/T\) is annihilated by an integer \(\nu > 0\). One can find finitely many elements \(g_i \in H(k)\) such that \[ H = \coprod_i g_i \cdot G, \] and we shall have \(g_i^\nu \in T(k)\). As k is algebraically closed, \(\nu \cdot \mathrm{id}_T\) is surjective on \(T(k) \simeq k^{*d}\), so after replacing the \(g_i\) by \(g_i t_i^{-1}\), where \(t_i \in T(k)\) is such that \(t_i^\nu = g_i^\nu\), we may assume that \(g_i^\nu = 1\). If then n is a multiple of \(\nu\), we shall have \[ {}_n\mathrm{H} \supseteq g_i \cdot {}_n\mathrm{G}, \] and as (for varying n > 0) the family of the \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is schematically dense in G by virtue of IX 4.7, the conclusion 3.6 appears.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.7

Let A be a noetherian ring equipped with an ideal I such that A is separated and complete for the I-adic topology, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(A/I)\), and let H be an affine S-scheme of finite type, flat over S at the points of \(H_0\), such that \(H_0 = H \times_S S_0\) is of multiplicative type and isotrivial. Then there exists an open and closed subgroup G of H, of multiplicative type, isotrivial (and of finite type), such that \(G_0 = H_0\).

By virtue of 3.2 (i), there exist an isotrivial group of multiplicative type G over S and an isomorphism \[ u_0 : G_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H_0. \] By virtue of 3.1, \(u_0\) comes from a unique homomorphism of S-groups \[ u : G \longrightarrow H. \] Using IX 6.6, one sees that u is a monomorphism (for if U is the set of \(s \in S\) such that \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is a monomorphism, then U is an open neighbourhood of \(S_0\), hence identical to S, and \(G|_U \to H|_U\) is a monomorphism). By virtue of IX 2.5, u is even a closed immersion.16 Therefore G is of finite type, hence of finite presentation over S. Then, by virtue of Lemma 3.5 in the "étale" case, one sees that there exists an open neighbourhood U of \(S_0\), hence identical to S, such that \(G|_U \to H|_U\) is étale, so u is étale, hence an open immersion (being an étale monomorphism17), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let A be a noetherian ring equipped with an ideal I such that A is separated and complete for the I-adic topology, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(A/I)\), and let H be an S-group prescheme of finite type, affine and flat over S. For H to be of multiplicative type and isotrivial, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H_0\) be so, and that H satisfy one of the following conditions a) and b) (which are therefore equivalent given the preceding conditions):

  1. a)

    The fibres of H are of multiplicative type, and of constant type on each connected component of S.

  2. b)

    H is commutative and the \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) (\(n > 0\)) are finite over S.

These last conditions are also implied by the following one:

  1. c)

    The fibres of H are connected.

Of course, if H is of multiplicative type (and isotrivial), the conditions a) and b) are satisfied, by IX 1.4 a) and 2.1 c), so only the "sufficiency" requires a proof. We shall use the subgroup G indicated in 3.7. When condition c) is satisfied, one obviously has \(G = H\) and we are done.

In case b), one notes that the immersion \(u : G \to H\) induces an open immersion \[ {}_n u : {}_n\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow {}_n\mathrm{H} \] which induces an isomorphism \(({}_n\mathrm{G})_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } ({}_n\mathrm{H})_0\); as \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is finite over S, this implies at once that \({}_n u\) is an isomorphism (the complement of its image is finite over S and reduces to \(\varnothing\)). By virtue of 3.6 it follows that the morphisms induced on the fibres \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) are isomorphisms, so u is surjective, hence an isomorphism.

Finally, in case a), we may assume S connected,18 and it follows that for every \(s \in S\), \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is a monomorphism of algebraic groups of multiplicative type and of the same type over \(\kappa(s)\).19 I claim that such a homomorphism is necessarily an isomorphism (which will again complete the proof20). Indeed, we may assume, after extending the base field, that the two groups over \(\kappa(s)\) are diagonalizable, and then this follows from VIII 3.2 b) and from the fact that a surjective homomorphism of isomorphic \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules of finite type \(M \to N\) is necessarily bijective.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9

Let A be a noetherian ring equipped with an ideal I such that A is separated and complete for the I-adic topology, and let H be an S-group prescheme of finite type, affine and flat over S, with connected fibres. For H to be an isotrivial torus, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H_0\) be so.

pages.title: 4. Case of an arbitrary base. Quasi-isotriviality theorem

blocks.html: 4. Case of an arbitrary base. Quasi-isotriviality theorem
Let A be a local ring. Recall that following Nagata one says that A is henselian if every algebra B finite over A is a product of local algebras \(B_i\) finite over A.

blocks.title: Rappel
blocks.html:
Reminder 4.0

21 Let A be a henselian local ring, k its residue field, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\), and \(\xi\) a geometric point of \(S_0\). Then the functor \[ X \longmapsto X_0 = X \times_S S_0 \] is an equivalence between the category of étale coverings of S and the analogous category over \(S_0\) (cf. EGA IV4, §18.5). Consequently (cf. SGA 1, V), we have \(\pi_1(S_0,\xi) = \pi_1(S,\xi)\).

Suppose moreover A noetherian and denote by \(A'\) its completion, \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(A')\). Then \(A'\) is a complete noetherian local ring, hence henselian (loc. cit., 18.5.14), and the functor \[ X \longmapsto X' = X \times_S S', \] from the category of étale coverings of S to the analogous category over \(S'\), fits into a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Rev.\acute{e}t.}(S) \ar[rr] \ar[dr]_-{\simeq} & & \mathrm{Rev.\acute{e}t.}(S') \ar[dl]^-{\simeq} \\ & \mathrm{Rev.\acute{e}t.}(S_0) & } \] hence is also an equivalence of categories, whence \(\pi_1(S_0,\xi) = \pi_1(S',\xi)\).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 4.0.1

As S is connected (A being local), it follows from 1.2 that the category of isotrivial groups of multiplicative type over S is equivalent to the analogous category over \(S_0\) (and also over \(S'\) if moreover A is noetherian).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1

Let A be a henselian local ring with residue field k, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\).

  1. (i)

    The functor \(H \mapsto H_0 = H \times_S S_0\) is an equivalence between the category of finite groups of multiplicative type over S and the analogous category over \(S_0\).

  2. (ii)

    If moreover A is noetherian, denoting by \(A'\) its completion and \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(A')\), the functor \(H \mapsto H' = H \times_S S'\) is an equivalence between the category of finite groups of multiplicative type over S and the analogous category over \(S'\).

As in 4.0, the second assertion is a consequence of the first; let us prove the latter. One already knows that the functor under consideration is essentially surjective, for every group of multiplicative type \(H_0\) over \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\), finite hence of finite type over k, is isotrivial by 1.4, hence comes from an isotrivial group of multiplicative type over S, by 4.0.1. It remains to prove full faithfulness, i.e. that for two finite groups G, H of multiplicative type over S, the map below is bijective: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0\text{-gr.}}(G_0,H_0), \] 22 that is, denoting by F the S-functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H)\), that the natural map \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(S,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S_0}(S_0,F_0) \] induced by the base change \(S_0 \to S\), is bijective. For this, in view of Reminder 4.0, it suffices to prove the

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2

Let G, H be two finite groups of multiplicative type over a prescheme S. Then \(\mathrm{F} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H)\) is representable by a scheme finite and étale over S.

23 Let \(f : S' \to S\) be a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism such that \(G'\) and \(H'\) are diagonalizable. It suffices to show that \(\mathrm{F}_{S'}\) is representable by a prescheme \(X'\) étale and finite (hence affine) over \(S'\), for the descent datum on \(X'\) relative to f (cf. VIII 1.7.2) will then be effective (by SGA 1 VIII, 2.1), whence the existence of an S-prescheme X such that \(X \times_S S' = X'\), which represents F, and is étale and finite over S (cf. SGA 1, V 5.7 and EGA IV4, 17.7.3 (ii)). We may therefore assume that \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\) and \(H = \mathbf{D}_S(N)\), where M and N are finite commutative groups (cf. VIII 2.1 c). Then \(K = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(N,M)\) is a finite abelian group and, by VIII 1.5, we have an isomorphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H) \simeq K_S, \] which completes the proof of 4.2 and hence of 4.1.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.0

24 Let S be a henselian local scheme, s its closed point, \(g : X \to S\) a morphism locally of finite type, x an isolated point of the fibre \(X_s\).

  1. (i)

    Then \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is finite over \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\). (In particular, if the residue extension \(\kappa(x)/\kappa(s)\) is trivial, then \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s} \to \mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is surjective, by Nakayama's lemma.)

  2. (ii)

    If moreover g is separated, then \(X' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X,x})\) is an open and closed subscheme of X, i.e. we have a decomposition into a disjoint sum \(X = X' \coprod X''\).

Proof. By the local form of Zariski's main theorem found in [Pes66], or [Ray70, Ch. IV, Th. 1], x possesses an open affine neighbourhood \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{B})\) of finite type and quasi-finite over \(A = \mathscr{O}_{S,s}\), and there exists an open immersion \(\mathrm{U} \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{C})\), where C is a finite A-algebra. (N. B. to reach this conclusion, one may also use Chevalley's semicontinuity theorem (EGA IV3, 13.1.4), then the form of Zariski's main theorem given in loc. cit., 8.12.8.) As A is henselian, Y is the disjoint sum of local schemes \(\mathrm{Y}_1,\ldots,\mathrm{Y}_n\), each finite over S, and the points of Y above s are the closed points \(y_1,\ldots,y_n\). Hence \(x = y_i\) for some i, and \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x} = \mathscr{O}_{U,x} = \mathrm{C}_i\) is finite over A. Moreover, \(X' = \mathrm{C}_i\) is an open subscheme of U, hence of X. Suppose moreover g separated. Then, as the morphism \(X' \to S\) is finite (\(\mathrm{C}_i\) being finite over A), so is the immersion \(X' \to X\) (cf. EGA II, 6.1.5 (v)), hence \(X'\) is also closed in X.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3

Let A be a henselian noetherian local ring, \(A'\) its completion, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(A')\), s the closed point of S, G and H two S-group preschemes, with G of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, H locally of finite type and separated over S, \(H_s\) of multiplicative type, and H flat over S at the points of \(H_s\). Let \(G', H'\) be deduced from G, H by the base change \(S' \to S\). Then the natural map below is bijective: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S'\text{-gr.}}(G',H'). \]

As \(S'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over S, one knows by SGA 1, VIII 5.2 that this map is a bijection from the first member onto the part of the second formed by the \(u' : G' \to H'\) such that the two inverse images \(u_1'', u_2'' : G'' \to H''\) of \(u'\) over \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) (by the projections \(\mathrm{pr}_1, \mathrm{pr}_2\) of \(S''\) onto \(S'\)) are equal. So everything comes down to proving that for every homomorphism of \(S'\)-groups \(u' : G' \to H'\), we have \(u_1'' = u_2''\). By virtue of the density theorem IX 4.8 it suffices to prove that \(u_1''\) and \(u_2''\) coincide on \({}_n\mathrm{G}''\) for every integer \(n > 0\). (N. B. we need here in an essential way the density theorem in a case where the base prescheme, here \(S''\), is not locally noetherian). This reduces us, replacing G by \({}_n\mathrm{G}\), to the case where there exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_G = 0\), hence G finite over S. Let likewise \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) be the kernel of the n-th power morphism \(\varphi_n : H \to H\) in H. (N. B. we have not assumed H commutative, so \(\varphi_n\) (resp. \({}_n\mathrm{H}\)) is not necessarily a homomorphism (resp. a subgroup of H).) Since \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is defined as the fibred product of \(\varphi_n : H \to H\) and the unit section \(\varepsilon : S \to H\), its formation commutes with any base change \(T \to S\), i.e. we have \(({}_n\mathrm{H})_T = {}_n(\mathrm{H}_T)\). 25 We denote with an index m on the right the reductions modulo \(\mathfrak{m}^{m+1}\), where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of A. Then \(H_m\) is flat over \(S_m\) (since H is so over S at the points of \(H_0\)); by 2.4, as \(H_0\) is of multiplicative type and of finite type, so is \(H_m\). Hence each \(({}_n\mathrm{H})_m = {}_n(\mathrm{H}_m)\) is a subgroup of \(H_m\), of multiplicative type, finite and flat over \(S_m\). In particular, \(({}_n\mathrm{H})_0 = {}_n(\mathrm{H}_0)\) is finite over \(S_0\); since S is henselian local and \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is (like H) locally of finite type and separated over S, then, by 4.3.0, the local rings of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) at the points of \(({}_n\mathrm{H})_0\) are finite over S and we have a decomposition into a disjoint sum of open sets \[ \text{(*)} \qquad {}_n\mathrm{H} = {}_n\mathrm{H}^+ \coprod {}_n\mathrm{H}^-, \] where \(\mathrm{Z} = {}_n\mathrm{H}^+\) is finite over S, and \({}_n\mathrm{H}^-\) lies above \(\mathrm{S} - \{s\}\). Note that, for every finite S-scheme Y (such as G), every S-morphism \(\mathrm{Y} \to {}_n\mathrm{H}\) factors through Z, and that the formation of the decomposition (*) commutes with the base change \(S' \to S\) (where \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(A')\), \(A'\) the completion of A). Then \(\mathrm{Z}' = \mathrm{Z} \times_S S'\) is a scheme finite over \(S'\), as is \(\mathrm{P}' = \mathrm{Z}' \times_{S'} \mathrm{Z}'\). Denote by \(\nu\) the restriction to \(\mathrm{P}'\) of the multiplication of \(H'\) and by \(\sigma\) the automorphism of \(\mathrm{P}'\) exchanging the two factors. As \(\mathrm{P}'\) is finite over \(S'\) and \(H'\) separated and locally of finite type over \(S'\), then, by EGA 5.4.3 and IV1 1.1.3, \(\mathrm{Y} = \nu(\mathrm{P}')\) is a closed subscheme of \(H'\), universally closed and quasi-compact, hence of finite type. Moreover, as \(S'\) is noetherian, the morphism \(\mathrm{Y} \to S'\) has finite fibres (cf. EGA III, 4.4.2). As \(\mathrm{Z}' \subseteq \mathrm{Y}\) and \(\mathrm{Z}'_m = \mathrm{Y}_m\) for all m, then \(\mathrm{Z}' = \mathrm{Y}\), by Lemma IX 5.0. Likewise, the kernel \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{Ker}(\nu, \nu \circ \sigma)\) is a closed subscheme of \(\mathrm{P}'\), such that \(\mathrm{K}_m = \mathrm{P}'_m\) for all m (since \(\mathrm{Z}'_m = {}_n\mathrm{H}_m\) is commutative), hence \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{P}'\), i.e. \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is a commutative subgroup of \(H'\). On the other hand, as each reduction \(\mathrm{Z}'_m = {}_n\mathrm{H}_m\) is flat over \(S_m\), then, by the "local criterion of flatness" (cf. EGA 0III, 10.2.2 or [BAC], III §5, Example 1 and Th. 1), \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is flat over \(S'\). As, moreover, \(\mathrm{Z}'_0 = {}_n\mathrm{H}_0\) is a group of multiplicative type finite over \(S_0\), hence isotrivial by 1.4, then \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is of multiplicative type (and isotrivial) over \(S'\), by 3.8 b). As \(S' \to S\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, one deduces that the multiplication \(\mathrm{Z} \times_S H \to H\) factors through Z and makes Z a subgroup of H, finite over S and of multiplicative type (since \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is). Finally, by the remarks made above on the decomposition (*), \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is the "finite part" of \({}_n\mathrm{H}'\), and therefore the morphism \(u' : G' \to {}_n\mathrm{H}'\) takes its values in \(\mathrm{Z}'\). As Z is of multiplicative type and finite over S, as is \(G = {}_n\mathrm{G}\), then, by 4.1, \(u'\) comes from a \(u : G \to \mathrm{Z}\), and hence \(u_1'' = u_2''\). This completes the proof of 4.3.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.0

26 Let A be a ring, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), H an S-group scheme of multiplicative type and of finite type, quasi-isotrivial (resp. isotrivial), \((A_i)\) a filtered family of subrings of A of which A is the inductive limit, \(S_i = \mathrm{Spec}(A_i)\). Then there exist an index i and an \(S_i\)-group scheme \(H_i\), of multiplicative type and of finite type, quasi-isotrivial (resp. isotrivial), such that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H}_i \times_{S_i} S\).

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4

Let S be a prescheme, H an S-group prescheme affine and of finite presentation over S, and \(s \in S\). We assume:

  1. α)

    H is flat over S at the points of \(H_s\).

  2. β)

    \(H_s\) is of multiplicative type.

Then there exist an étale morphism \(S' \to S\), a point \(s'\) of \(S'\) above s such that the extension \(\kappa(s')/\kappa(s)\) is trivial, and an open and closed subgroup \(G'\) of \(H' = H \times_S S'\), of multiplicative type and of finite type, isotrivial, such that \(G'_{s'} = H'_{s'}\).

27 a) Denote provisionally \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S,s})\) and let us show that, by "descent of the conclusions", we can reduce to the case where \(S = T\). Indeed, suppose we have found an étale morphism \(g : T' \to T\), a point \(s' \in T'\) and a subgroup \(G'\) of \(H' = H_{T'}\) satisfying the conditions of the statement; replacing \(T'\) by an open affine neighbourhood of \(s'\), we may assume \(T'\) of finite presentation over T. As \(G'\) is isotrivial, there exists a finite surjective étale morphism \(f : \widetilde{T} \to T'\) such that \(\widetilde{G} = G' \times_{T'} \widetilde{T}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{D}_{\widetilde{T}}(M)\), where M is an abelian group of finite type. Since \(T', H\), and \(\widetilde{T}, G'\) are of finite presentation over \(T = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S,s})\), and \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\) is the inductive limit of the subalgebras \(A_i = \mathscr{O}_S(S_i)\), where \(S_i\) runs through the open affine neighbourhoods of s in S, then, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2 (and Exp. VIB, 10.2 and 10.3), there exist an index i, \(S_i\)-preschemes (resp. an \(S_i\)-prescheme) of finite presentation \(S_i'\) and \(\widetilde{S}_i\) (resp. \(G_i'\)), and morphisms \(g_i : S_i' \to S_i\) and \(f_i : \widetilde{S}_i \to S_i'\) (resp. a morphism of \(S_i\)-group preschemes \(u_i : G_i' \to H \times_S S_i'\)), from which f and g (resp. \(u : G' \to H'\)) come by the base change \(T' \to S_i\). Moreover, taking i large enough, \(g_i\) will be étale, \(f_i\) finite étale surjective (cf. EGA IV3, 8.10.5 and 10.3). Then \(\widetilde{G}\) comes from the groups \(\widetilde{G}_i = G_i' \times_{S_i'} \widetilde{S}_i\) and \(\mathbf{D}_{\widetilde{S}_i}(M)\); hence, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2 (i) (and VIB, 10.2), there exists an index \(j \geqslant i\) such that \(\widetilde{G}_j \simeq \mathbf{D}_{\widetilde{S}_j}(M)\), so \(G_j\) is of multiplicative type and isotrivial. Denote by \(s_j'\) the image of \(s'\) in \(S_j'\). Then the étale morphism \(S_j' \to S_j\), the point \(s_j'\) and the open and closed subgroup \(G_j'\) of \(H \times_S S_j'\), satisfy the conditions of the statement. This shows that we may assume S = \(\mathrm{Spec}(A)\) local, with closed point s. b) Then A is the inductive limit of local rings \(A_i\) which are localizations of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras of finite type; denote \(S_i = \mathrm{Spec}(A_i)\). Let us show that the hypotheses α) and β) "descend" to some \(A_i\). As \(H \to S\) is of finite presentation, the set of \(x \in H\) such that H is flat over S at x is an open set W, which contains \(H_s\) by hypothesis, hence contains a quasi-compact open V containing \(H_s\) (for \(H_s\) being affine, one may cover it by finitely many open affines contained in W). Then the open immersion \(\tau : V \hookrightarrow H\) is of finite presentation, so \(V \to S\) is too. Therefore, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2 (and Exp. VIB, 10.2 and 10.3), there exist an index i, an \(S_i\)-prescheme \(V_i\) (resp. an \(S_i\)-group prescheme \(H_i\)) of finite presentation over \(S_i\), and an \(S_i\)-morphism \(\tau_i : V_i \to H_i\) from which V, H and \(\tau\) come by the base change \(S \to S_i\); moreover, taking i large enough, \(\tau_i\) will be an open immersion and \(V_i\) will be flat over \(S_i\), by EGA IV3, 8.10.5 and 11.2.6. Denote by \(s_i\) the image of s in \(S_i\); as the open immersion \((V_i)_{s_i} \hookrightarrow (H_i)_{s_i}\) gives, by the base change \(\kappa(s_i) \to \kappa(s)\), the equality \(V_s = H_s\), we get \((H_i)_{s_i} \subseteq V_i\), so \(H_i\) is flat over \(S_i\) at the points of \((H_i)_{s_i}\). Finally, \((H_i)_{s_i}\) is of multiplicative type, since \(H_s = (H_i)_{s_i} \otimes_{\kappa(s_i)} \kappa(s)\) is. So the triple \((S_i,H_i,s_i)\) satisfies the hypotheses of 4.4, and if the desired assertion holds for this triple, it will also hold, by base change, for \((S,H,s)\). This reduces us to the case where A is local and noetherian. Let us now distinguish two cases.

1° A is local noetherian and henselian. Let \(\widehat{A}\) be its completion, \(\widehat{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(\widehat{A})\), and \(\widehat{H} = H \times_S \widehat{S}\). Applying Theorem 3.7, one finds an \(\widehat{S}\)-group \(\widehat{G}\) of multiplicative type, isotrivial and of finite type, and a homomorphism of \(\widehat{S}\)-groups \[ \widehat{u} : \widehat{G} \longrightarrow \widehat{H} \] which is an open immersion and a closed immersion, such that \(\widehat{u}\) induces an isomorphism \(\widehat{u}_0 : \widehat{G}_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \widehat{H}_0\). By Remark 4.0.1, the base-change functor by \(\widehat{S} \to S\) induces an equivalence between the categories of isotrivial groups of multiplicative type over S and over \(\widehat{S}\); in particular \(\widehat{G}\) "comes" from an S-group of multiplicative type G, isotrivial and of finite type. By virtue of 4.3, \(\widehat{u}\) comes from a homomorphism \[ u : G \longrightarrow H\text{;} \] moreover u is an open and closed immersion and induces an isomorphism \(u_0 : G_0 \to H_0\), since this is so after the faithfully flat quasi-compact base change \(\widehat{S} \to S\). This therefore proves 4.4 in this case (taking of course, in the conclusion of 4.4, \(S' = S\) and \(s' = s\)).

2° A is local noetherian. The reduction to case 1° is immediate, by applying 1° to the "henselized" ring \(A^h\) of A. More precisely, one sees easily (using SGA 1, I §5(*)) that the local rings \(\mathscr{O}_{S',s'}\) of the étale S-preschemes \(S'\) equipped with a point \(s'\) above s such that \(\kappa(s')/\kappa(s)\) is trivial, form a filtered inductive system, whose inductive limit is a henselian noetherian local ring \(A^h\) (called the "henselized" ring of A); for details of this construction (due to Nagata in the normal case), cf. SGA 4, VIII §4(*). The sorites of EGA IV3 §8 then make it possible, as in part a) of the proof, to deduce from a known result on the inductive limit \(A^h\) of the \(\mathscr{O}_{S',s'}\), an analogous result on one of the \((S',s')\), which proves 4.4.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5

Let S be a prescheme, H an S-group prescheme of multiplicative type and of finite type. Then H is quasi-isotrivial, i.e. is split by a surjective étale morphism \(S' \to S\).

Indeed, let \(s \in S\). By 4.4, there exist an étale morphism \(S' \to S\), an \(s' \in S'\) above s such that \(\kappa(s) = \kappa(s')\), and a subgroup \(G'\) of \(H'\), of multiplicative type, isotrivial and of finite type, such that \(G'_{s'} = H'_{s'}\). As \(G'\) and \(H'\) are of multiplicative type and of finite type, then, by IX 2.9, there exists an open neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(s'\) such that \(G'|_{U'} = H'|_{U'}\). 28 Suppose moreover S henselian local, with closed point s; then, by EGA IV4, 18.5.11 b), there exists a section \(\sigma\) of \(S' \to S\) such that \(\sigma(s) = s'\). (N. B. one can see directly that \(\mathrm{B} = \mathscr{O}_{S',s'}\) equals \(A = \mathscr{O}_{S,s}\) as follows: by 4.3.0 (i), we have \(\mathrm{B} \simeq A/I\), and as B is an A-algebra of finite presentation and flat, I is an ideal of finite type (cf. EGA IV1, 1.4.7), and \(I = I\mathfrak{m}\) (where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of A), whence \(I = 0\).) So H is already isotrivial. One therefore obtains:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6

Let A be a henselian local ring, k its residue field, and \(\pi\) the topological Galois group of an algebraic closure of k.

  1. (i)

    Every group of multiplicative type and of finite type over \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\) is isotrivial.

  2. (ii)

    The category of these groups over S is equivalent (via the functor \(H \mapsto H \otimes_A k\)) to the analogous category over \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\); it is therefore anti-equivalent, by 1.4, to the category of Galois modules under \(\pi\) that are of finite type as \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7

Under the conditions of 4.4 suppose moreover that one of the following conditions is satisfied:

  1. a)

    For every generization t of s, \(H_t\) is of multiplicative type and of the same type as \(H_s\).

  2. b)

    H is commutative and the \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) (\(n > 0\)) are finite over S in a neighbourhood of s.

  3. c)

    For every generization t of s, the fibre \(H_t\) is connected.

Then there exists an open neighbourhood U of s such that \(H|_U\) is of multiplicative type.

Taking into account that an étale morphism is open, we are reduced to proving that there exists (with the notation of the conclusion of 4.4) an open neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(s'\) such that \(G'|_{U'} = H'|_{U'}\). Set \(S'' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S',s'})\); as \(G'\) and \(H'\) are of finite presentation over \(S'\), it suffices to show, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2, that \(G'' = H''\). We may therefore assume \(S = S''\), and then the hypotheses (a), (b), (c) become those of the lemma below, whose proof is the same as that of 3.8:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.7.1

Let S be a local scheme, s its closed point, H an S-group scheme of finite type, G an open and closed subgroup of H, of multiplicative type, such that \(G_s = H_s\). Assume moreover that one of the following conditions is satisfied:

  1. a)

    The fibres of H are of multiplicative type and of the same type as \(H_s\).

  2. b)

    H is commutative and the \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) (\(n > 0\)) are finite over S.

  3. c)

    The fibres of H are connected.

Then \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{H}\).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8

Let S be a prescheme, H an S-group prescheme affine, flat and of finite presentation over S, with fibres of multiplicative type. For H to be of multiplicative type, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfy one of the two following conditions (equivalent given the preceding conditions):

  1. a)

    The type of \(H_s\) (cf. IX 1.4) is a locally constant function of \(s \in S\).

  2. b)

    H is commutative, and the \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) (\(n > 0\)) are finite over S.

Moreover, these conditions are implied by the following one:

  1. c)

    The fibres of H are connected.

In particular, one finds:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9

Let S be a prescheme, H an S-group prescheme flat and of finite presentation over S. Suppose moreover H affine over S and with connected fibres.29 If \(s \in S\) is such that \(H_s\) is a torus, there exists an open neighbourhood U of s such that \(H|_U\) is a torus. In particular, if all the fibres of H are tori, H is a torus.

pages.title: 5. Scheme of homomorphisms from one group of multiplicative type to another. Twisted constant groups and groups of multiplicative type

blocks.html: 5. Scheme of homomorphisms from one group of multiplicative type to another. Twisted constant groups and groups of multiplicative type

blocks.title: Définition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1

a) Let S be a prescheme, R a group prescheme over S. One says that R is a twisted constant group over S if it is locally isomorphic, in the sense of the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, to a constant group scheme, i.e. one of the form \(M_S\) with M an ordinary group.

b) One says that the twisted constant group R over S is quasi-isotrivial, resp. isotrivial, resp. locally isotrivial, resp. locally trivial, resp. trivial, if in the above definition one can replace the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology by the étale topology, resp. the global finite étale topology, resp. the Zariski topology, resp. the coarsest (or "chaotic") topology, cf. IX 1.1 and 1.2, and IV 6.6. To say that R is quasi-isotrivial (resp. isotrivial) therefore means that there exists a surjective (resp. and finite) étale morphism \(S' \to S\) such that \(R' = R \times_S S'\) is a constant group over S; to say that it is trivial means that R is a constant group.

c) One defines as in VIII 1.4 the type of a twisted constant group R over S at an \(s \in S\); it is a class of ordinary groups up to isomorphism, which, for varying R, is a locally constant function of s, hence constant if S is connected. One will also say that R is "of type M" if all the fibres of R are of type M. One should take care that R is quasi-compact over S only if it is finite over S, i.e. if its type at every \(s \in S\) is a finite group.30

d) The most interesting case for us is that where R is commutative. We shall then say that R is "finitely generated" if its type at every point \(s \in S\) is given by a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module of finite type, a notion which must not be confused with the schematic notion "R of finite type over S" (cf. above).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.2

We shall also have to consider S-preschemes X which are locally isomorphic (for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology) to constant preschemes, independently of any group structure. We shall then say that X is a twisted constant bundle over S, and shall extend to these preschemes the terminology introduced in 5.1. Of course, one should take care that when X is equipped with an S-group structure, the meaning of the expressions "twisted constant", "isotrivial" etc. changes, according to whether or not one takes into account the group structure over S. The same holds if one considers on X any other kind of algebraic structure (for example that of a Galois principal bundle, which will be considered in the next section).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.3

Let R be a commutative twisted constant group over S.

  1. (i)

    The functor \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\) (cf. VIII 1) is representable and is a group of multiplicative type over S.

  2. (ii)

    For every \(s \in S\), the type of R at s is equal to that of H at s.

  3. (iii)

    For R to be quasi-isotrivial (resp. isotrivial, resp. trivial, resp. locally isotrivial, resp. locally trivial) it is necessary and sufficient that H be so.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.3.1

31 One may perhaps be worried at seeing the term "type" used in 5.3 in two different senses according to whether R or H is concerned; fortunately, when an S-group prescheme G is both a twisted constant group and a group of multiplicative type, its type in either sense is the same, thanks to the fact that a finite ordinary commutative group is isomorphic to its dual!

Proof. As the covering families for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology are effective descent families for the fibred category of affine groups over variable base preschemes (SGA 1, VIII 2.1), one sees that H is representable (and is affine over S), since it is so "locally"32 (for it is so when R is constant, and then H is a diagonalizable group). The fact that H is of multiplicative type is then obvious by definition, as is the fact that the type of R and of H at \(s \in S\) is the same. Finally, since \(H_{S'} = \mathbf{D}_{S'}(R_{S'})\), the last assertion is reduced to the "trivial" case, i.e. to verifying that R is trivial if and only if H is, which follows at once from the biduality theorem VIII 1.2. To finish making precise the correspondence between twisted constant groups and groups of multiplicative type, one must start from a group of multiplicative type H, and study \(\mathrm{R} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{H})\). If the latter is representable, it is obviously a twisted constant group, and we shall have \(\mathrm{H} \simeq \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\). In other words:

blocks.title: Scholie
blocks.html:
Scholium 5.4.0

The functor \(\mathrm{R} \mapsto \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\) is an anti-equivalence33 between the category of twisted constant groups over S and that of the groups of multiplicative type H over S such that \(\mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{H})\) is representable.
I do not know whether this condition on H is always satisfied; we shall see however that it is when H is quasi-isotrivial, in particular when H is of finite type.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.4

Let \(S' \to S\) be a faithfully flat morphism locally of finite presentation, \(X'\) an \(S'\)-prescheme separated, locally of finite presentation and locally quasi-finite over \(S'\). Then every descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(S' \to S\) is effective, i.e. there exist an S-prescheme X, and an \(S'\)-isomorphism \(X \times_S S' \simeq X'\) compatible with the descent datum.

We have already remarked that the hypothesis on \(S' \to S\) implies that it is a covering morphism for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology (IV 6.3.1 (iv)), a fortiori an effective descent morphism. When \(X' \to S'\) is quasi-compact, hence of finite presentation and quasi-finite, then it is a quasi-affine morphism (cf. SGA 1, VIII 6.234), and effectivity follows in this case from SGA 1, VIII 7.9. In the general case, one reduces at once to the case where S and \(S'\) are affine. One covers \(X'\) by open affines \(U_i'\); let \(V_i'\) be the saturation of \(U_i'\) for the equivalence relation in \(X'\) defined by the descent datum, i.e. \(V_i' = q_2(q_1^{-1}(U_i'))\), where \(q_1, q_2\) are the two projections of \(X_1'' = X' \times_{S'} (S'',p_1)\) onto \(X'\) (\(q_1 = \mathrm{pr}_1\), and \(q_2\) is deduced from the first projection of \(X_2'' = X' \times_{S'}(S'',p_2)\) thanks to the given descent isomorphism \(X_1'' \simeq X_2''\)). As \(S' \to S\) is faithfully flat, quasi-compact and locally of finite presentation, the same holds for \(p_1 : S'' = S' \times_S S' \to S'\), hence also for \(q_1\) and \(q_2\), which are consequently open morphisms (SGA 1 IV 6.635). Consequently, \(V_i'\) is an open and quasi-compact part of \(X'\). By what we have already seen, the descent data induced in the \(V_i'\) are effective, from which it follows that the same holds for that of \(X'\) (SGA 1, VIII 7.2).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

A faithfully flat morphism \(S' \to S\) of finite presentation is an effective descent morphism for the fibred category of twisted constant groups (over variable base preschemes).
Indeed, this amounts to asserting the effectivity of a descent datum under the conditions of 5.4, when \(X'\) is a constant \(S'\)-prescheme.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.6

Let S be a prescheme, G and H two S-group preschemes of multiplicative type, quasi-isotrivial, with G36 of finite type. Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G)\) is representable by, and is a quasi-isotrivial twisted constant group over S;37 for every \(s \in S\), if the type at s of G (resp. H) is M (resp. N), that of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G)\) is \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,N)\).

One proceeds as in 4.2, using the fact that the assertion is established (VIII 1.5) when G and H are trivial. The necessary effectivity criterion is provided by 5.5 (in the case of a surjective étale morphism \(S' \to S\)). In particular, taking \(G = \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\), one finds:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7
  1. (i)

    Let H be a quasi-isotrivial S-group of multiplicative type; then the S-group \(\mathbf{D}_S(H)\) is representable and is a quasi-isotrivial twisted constant group over S.

  2. (ii)

    The functors \(\mathrm{R} \mapsto \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\) and \(\mathrm{H} \mapsto \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{H})\) are anti-equivalences, quasi-inverse to one another, between the category of quasi-isotrivial twisted constant S-groups and that of quasi-isotrivial S-groups of multiplicative type.

  3. (iii)

    These functors induce anti-equivalences between the subcategories formed by the isotrivial, resp. locally isotrivial, resp. locally trivial, resp. trivial groups.

The last assertion is included only for the record, being already contained in 5.3. Moreover, as every S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type is quasi-isotrivial, by 4.5, one deduces from 5.6:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8

Let S be a prescheme, G and H two S-group preschemes of multiplicative type and of finite type; then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G)\) is representable and is a quasi-isotrivial twisted constant S-group over S.

Note also that in 5.3, R is finitely generated if and only if \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\) is of finite type (IX 2.1 b)). By virtue of 4.5, H is then quasi-isotrivial, hence R is quasi-isotrivial. One thus finds:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.9

The functors of 5.7 induce anti-equivalences, quasi-inverse to one another, between the category of S-groups H of multiplicative type of finite type, and that of finitely generated twisted constant S-groups R; moreover, every such group R is quasi-isotrivial.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10

Let H, G be two S-group preschemes of multiplicative type and of finite type.

  1. (i)

    Then \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G)\) is representable by an open and closed subprescheme of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G)\), and it is a twisted constant S-prescheme.

  2. (ii)

    In particular, \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H)\) is representable and is a twisted constant S-group (in general non-commutative).

This follows from 5.8 and VIII 1.6.38

blocks.title: Rappel
blocks.html:
Reminder 5.11.0

39 Recall that if X is a locally noetherian prescheme, its connected components (which are always closed) are open. Indeed, let C be a connected component of X, \(x \in C\) and U a noetherian open neighbourhood of x; then U has only finitely many connected components, so the connected component \(U_x\) of x in U is open in U hence in X; as \(U_x \subseteq C\), this shows that C is open in X.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.11

Let S be a prescheme, R a commutative twisted constant group over S, \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\) the group of multiplicative type it defines. Consider the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    H is isotrivial (i.e. R is isotrivial).

  2. (ii)

    R is a union of subpreschemas both open and closed \(R_i\), which are quasi-compact over S (and then necessarily finite over S).

  3. (iii)

    The connected components of R are finite over S.

a) Then (i) ⇒ (ii) ⇒ (iii).40

b) One has (i) ⇔ (ii) ⇔ (iii) if S is locally noetherian.

c) Finally, (i) ⇔ (ii) if R is finitely generated (i.e. if H is of finite type over S), at least if S is quasi-compact or if its connected components are open.

Decomposing first S into a prescheme sum of preschemas \(S_i\) on each of which H is of constant type (cf. IX 1.4.1), we are reduced to the case where H, hence R, is of constant type M. We shall need the

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.12

Let S be a prescheme, R a twisted constant prescheme over S. Then every closed subprescheme Z of R which is quasi-compact over S is finite over S.

Indeed, we are reduced to the case where R is constant, hence of the form \(I_S\), where I is a set, hence the filtered increasing union of the \(J_S\), where J runs through the finite subsets of I. We may assume moreover S affine; then Z is quasi-compact, hence contained in one of the \(J_S\). As \(J_S\) is finite over S, so is Z. Lemma 5.12 already establishes the parenthetical assertion in 5.11 (ii).41 The implication (ii) ⇒ (iii) is then clear, since the connected components of R are closed; by 5.11.0 they are open and closed if S is locally noetherian (R being étale, hence locally of finite type over S), whence (iii) ⇒ (ii) in this case. Let us prove that (i) ⇒ (ii). For this, let \(S' \to S\) be a finite surjective étale morphism that splits H, hence R (cf. 5.3), so that \(R'\) is isomorphic to \(M_{S'} = \coprod_{m \in M} R'_m\), where the \(R'_m\) are disjoint open sets of \(R'\), \(S'\)-isomorphic to \(S'\). Let \(g : R' \to R\) be the projection, which is a finite étale surjective morphism, hence an open and closed morphism; then the \(R_m = g(R'_m)\) are parts of R both open and closed, and obviously quasi-compact over S since the \(R'_m\) are. Finally, suppose H of finite type over S, and let us prove (ii) ⇒ (i). The case where the connected components of S are open reduces at once to the case where S is quasi-compact or connected. As M is finitely generated, we can write \(M = \mathbb{Z}^r \times N\), where r is an integer \(\geqslant 0\) and N a finite abelian group. Let \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\); consider the preschemas \[ \mathrm{P} = \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) = \mathrm{Q} \] cf. 5.8 and 5.10. We have isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{Q} \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S,R) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathbb{Z}^r_S,R) \times \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(N_S,R) \simeq R^r \times \mathrm{E}, \] where \(\mathrm{E} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(N_S,R)\) is finite over S.42 It follows that Q is a union of subpreschemas both open and closed \(Q_i\), finite over S. Hence P is a union of the subpreschemas both open and closed \(P_i = P \cap Q_i\), finite over S. As they are étale over S, their images in S are parts \(S_i\) both open and closed, and they cover S. If S is connected or quasi-compact, there exists therefore a finite set of indices i such that the \(S_i\) cover S; let \(S'\) be the union of the corresponding \(P_i\). Then \(S' \to S\) is finite étale surjective, and setting \(P' = P \times_S S' = \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{S'\text{-gr.}}(H',G')\), one sees that \(P'\) has a section over \(S'\), so there exists an isomorphism of \(S'\)-groups \[ H' = H \times_S S' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } G' = G \times_S S' = \mathbf{D}_{S'}(M), \] which proves that \(S'\) splits H. This completes the proof of 5.11.43

blocks.label: 5.11 bis
blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.11 bis

Let us remark moreover that one can, when H is of finite type over S and of constant type, give the following isotriviality criterion (where it is no longer necessary to make any restriction on S): \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{R})\) is isotrivial if and only if R is the union of a sequence of parts both open and closed, finite over S.44

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.13

Let S be a locally noetherian and connected prescheme, P a quasi-isotrivial twisted constant S-prescheme, Z a part of P both open and closed, such that there exists an \(s \in S\) such that \(Z_s\) is finite. Then Z is finite over S.

Let us not assume at first that S is connected: let U be the set of \(s \in S\) such that \(Z_s\) is finite; we shall prove that U is both open and closed, and that \(Z|_U\) is finite over U. This is obviously a statement essentially equivalent to 5.13 but which has the advantage of being "of a local nature" on S for the étale topology (say), which reduces us to the case where P is constant, i.e. I is a set. (N. B. the locally noetherian hypothesis is not lost under an étale base change; this is where one uses the quasi-isotriviality of P over S). We may moreover assume S connected, since its connected components are open (S being locally noetherian). But then we necessarily have \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{J}_S\), where J is a subset of I, and so \(\mathrm{U} = \varnothing\) or \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{S}\), according to whether J is infinite or finite, which gives the desired conclusion.

blocks.title: Rappels
blocks.html:
Reminders 5.14.0

45 Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, and let \(\widetilde{S}\) be the normalization of \(S_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) (cf. EGA II, 6.3.8). Recall that S is said to be geometrically unibranch (cf. EGA 0IV, §23.2 and §6.15) if the canonical morphism \(\widetilde{S} \to S\) is radicial (and hence a universal homeomorphism); in particular, the connected components of S are irreducible. Suppose then S connected, hence irreducible, let \(\eta\) be its generic point and let \(f : P \to S\) be a flat and locally quasi-finite morphism. Let \(P_i\) be the irreducible components of P, and \(\xi_i\) the generic point of \(P_i\); it lies above \(\eta\) (cf. EGA IV2, 2.3.4), and therefore \((P_i)_\eta = P_i \cap P_\eta\) is the closure of \(\xi_i\) in \(P_\eta\). Since the fibre \(P_\eta\) is discrete, we thus have \((P_i)_\eta = \{\xi_i\}\). This applies in particular when f is étale; in this case, P is also locally noetherian and geometrically unibranch (cf. EGA IV4, 17.5.7), so its irreducible components are its connected components and are open (and closed).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.14

Let S be a locally noetherian and geometrically unibranch prescheme, P a quasi-isotrivial twisted constant S-prescheme. Then the connected components of P are finite over S.

We may obviously assume S connected hence irreducible; let \(\eta\) be its generic point. By what precedes, each connected component \(P_i\) of P is open and closed, and meets the fibre \(P_\eta\) in a single point. Hence 5.13 applies and shows that each \(P_i\) is finite over S.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.16

46 Let S be a locally noetherian and geometrically unibranch prescheme. Then every S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type is isotrivial.

Indeed, we may assume S connected, hence H of constant type M. It suffices to apply 5.14 to \(\mathrm{P} = \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G)\), where \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\), then to reason as in the proof of 5.11 (ii) ⇒ (i). One can also apply 5.14 to \(\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{R} = \mathbf{D}_S(\mathrm{H})\) (cf. 5.9), then use 5.11.
