pages.title: 6. Infinite Galois principal coverings and enlarged fundamental group

blocks.html: 6. Infinite Galois principal coverings and enlarged fundamental group
(The results of the present section and of the next one will not be used again in the remainder of this Seminar).

Let S be a prescheme; we propose to determine the principal homogeneous bundles P over S with structure group of the form \(G_S\), the constant S-group defined by an ordinary group G (not necessarily finite), which will also be called Galois principal bundles over S with group G. We take "principal bundle" in the sense of the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology (cf. Exp. IV, Def. 5.1.5), but one will note that for such a P, the structural morphism \(P \to S\) is necessarily étale and surjective, hence covering for the étale topology; consequently P is also locally trivial for the étale topology (cf. IV, Prop. 5.1.6).47 We shall assume that S is a sum of connected preschemes, i.e. that its connected components are open, which reduces us at once to the case where S is connected. We shall then choose a "geometric point" \(\xi\) of S, i.e. an S-scheme \(\xi\) which is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(\Omega = \kappa(\xi)\). Then for every Galois principal bundle P over S with group G, \(P_\xi\) is a Galois principal bundle over the algebraically closed field \(\kappa(\xi)\), hence is trivial. We shall therefore refine the initial problem by proposing to determine the category of Galois principal bundles over S pointed above \(\xi\), i.e. equipped with an S-homomorphism \(\xi \to P\), i.e. with a trivialization of \(P_\xi\). For fixed G, the set of classes of such bundles, up to isomorphism respecting the base point, will be denoted \(\pi^1(S,\xi;G)\). Then the set \(\pi^1(S;G)\) of isomorphism classes of Galois principal bundles over S with group G (without a specified base point) is isomorphic to the set of orbits of G in \(\pi^1(S,\xi;G)\) (taking into account the natural operations of G on this set, corresponding to translation by G of the marked point in the pointed Galois principal bundle P): \[ \pi^1(S;G) = \pi^1(S,\xi;G)/G. \]

For every morphism \(S' \to S\) which is a universal effective descent morphism for the fibred category of twisted constant preschemes over a variable base (for example \(S' \to S\) faithfully flat and locally of finite presentation, cf. 5.4; for other examples cf. SGA 1 IX), we propose to determine the subsets of the preceding sets, denoted \(\pi^1(S'/S,\xi;G)\) and \(\pi^1(S'/S;G)\), formed by the Galois principal bundles over S which become trivial over \(S'\) (or, as one says, are "split" by \(S'\)). We shall in fact determine the category of Galois principal bundles P over S which are split by \(S'\). Of course, we shall then have \[ \pi^1(S,\xi;G) = \varinjlim_{S'} \pi^1(S'/S,\xi;G), \] where in the second member, \(S'\) runs through a cofinal system in the set of \(S'/S\) covering for the étale topology (for example, when S is quasi-compact, the set of \(S'\) over S which are quasi-compact and whose structural morphism is étale and surjective). Likewise, the category of Galois principal bundles over S will be the inductive limit of the subcategories defined by the \(S'\) (formed by the bundles that are split over \(S'\)). Thanks to the hypothesis made on \(S'/S\), the category of Galois principal bundles over S split by \(S'\) is equivalent to the category of trivial Galois principal bundles over \(S'\) (hence of the form \(G_{S'}\), where G operates by right translations), equipped with a descent datum relative to \(S' \to S\). The datum of a base point on a Galois principal bundle P over S split by \(S'\) amounts, in terms of the corresponding trivial bundle \(P'\) over \(S'\) and its descent datum, to the datum of a trivialization of \(P' \times_{S'} S'_\xi\) compatible with the induced descent datum, relative to \(S'_\xi \to \xi\) (N. B. we have set \(S'_\xi = S' \times_S \xi\)), i.e. a section \(\sigma\) of \(P'_\xi\) over \(S'_\xi\) compatible with the descent datum. It is then advantageous, for an arbitrary S-prescheme \(S'\) (for which one no longer assumes that \(S' \to S\) is a universal effective descent morphism for the fibred category of twisted constant bundles …) to define \(\pi^1(S'/S;G)\) and \(\pi^1(S'/S,\xi;G)\) as the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of the structures with descent data just specified. One then obtains for these functors in G a quite simple simplicial description, in terms of the fibred square and cube \(S''\) and \(S'''\) of \(S'\) over S, which we shall sketch below (cf. 6.3). The important conclusion to keep in mind will be the following:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.1

Suppose that the connected components of \(S'\) and \(S''\) are open, and, for example, that the quotient set of \(\pi_0(S')\) by the equivalence relation induced by the two projections \(\pi_0(S'') \to \pi_0(S')\) is a single point.48

  1. (i)

    The functor \(G \mapsto \pi^1(S'/S,\xi;G)\), from the category of groups to the category of sets, is representable by a group, denoted \(\pi_1(S'/S,\xi)\) and called the fundamental group of S at \(\xi\) relative to \(S' \to S\). We thus have a functorial bijection: \[ \pi^1(S'/S,\xi;G) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(\pi_1(S'/S,\xi),G). \]

  2. (ii)

    This group has a set of generators in bijection with \(\pi_0(S'')\), and is described in terms of these generators by relations in bijection with the elements of \(\pi_0(S''')\)49. In particular, \(\pi_1(S'/S,\xi)\) is finitely generated (resp. of finite presentation) if \(\pi_0(S'')\) (resp. together with \(\pi_0(S''')\)) is finite.

  3. (iii)

    The category of Galois principal bundles over S split by \(S'\), with base point above \(\xi\), is equivalent to the category of ordinary groups G, equipped with a homomorphism from \(\pi_1(S'/S,\xi)\) to G.

The proof is given below, cf. …

When S is a connected locally noetherian prescheme, which implies that every prescheme \(S'\) étale over S is locally noetherian and hence has open connected components, one concludes from what precedes50 that the functor \(G \mapsto \pi^1(S,\xi;G)\), from the category of ordinary groups to the category of sets, is strictly pro-representable (cf. Séminaire Bourbaki, February 1960, N° 195, §§ A.2 and A.3), i.e. there exist a projective system \[ \Pi = \Pi_1(S;\xi) = (\pi_i)_{i \in I} \] of ordinary groups over a filtered increasing index set I, which is "strict" (i.e. with surjective transition morphisms \(\pi_j \to \pi_i\)), and an isomorphism of functors in G \[ \pi^1(S,\xi;G) \simeq \varprojlim_i \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(\pi_i,G). \] The second member is also simply denoted \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{pro\text{-}gr.}}(\Pi,G)\) (cf. loc. cit.). In the case where the projective limit \(\pi = \varprojlim \pi_i\) is "large enough", precisely when the canonical homomorphisms \(\Pi \to \Pi_i\) are surjective, it is appropriate to equip \(\Pi\) with the projective limit topology of the discrete topologies of the \(\Pi_i\), and the preceding isomorphism can also be written: \[ \pi^1(S,\xi;G) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.top.}}(\pi,G), \] where the second member denotes the set of homomorphisms of topological groups, it being understood that G is equipped with the discrete topology. The hypothesis just formulated on the projective system \(\Pi\) is satisfied, as is well known, when the \(\pi_i\) are finite groups (cf. [BEns], III §7.4, Th. 1). This last condition obviously also means that every Galois principal bundle over S is isotrivial, i.e. is split by a finite surjective étale morphism. This is the case when S is geometrically unibranch (for example normal), as follows at once from 5.14.51 In the case where the \(\pi_i\) are finite, the group \(\pi\) also coincides with the fundamental group \(\pi_1(S,\xi)\) introduced in SGA 1, V. Thus, in the favourable case (\(\pi \to \pi_i\) surjective) one could call \(\pi\) the enlarged fundamental group of S at \(\xi\). Outside this favourable case, \(\pi\) itself is of hardly any interest, and the role of the usual fundamental group is played by the projective system \(\Pi\) itself, which will be called the enlarged fundamental pro-group of S at \(\xi\). (Any terminological suggestion better than "enlarged" is welcome!).52 One will note that the knowledge of this pro-group is more precise than that of the usual fundamental group \(\pi_1(S,\xi)\) of SGA 1 V; precisely, the latter is the projective limit of the projective system formed by the finite quotients of the \(\pi_i\).

Let us indicate quickly the "computation" of \(\pi_1(S'/S,\xi)\). Let \(S_i\) be the \((i+1)\)-th fibred power of \(S'\) over S (i.e. \(S_0 = S'\), \(S_1 = S''\), etc.). Between the \(S_i\) there are obvious simplicial operations, which make \((S_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}}\) a simplicial object of \((\mathbf{Sch})_{/S}\). Transforming this simplicial object by the functor "set of connected components" \[ \pi_0 : (\mathbf{Sch})_{/S} \longrightarrow (\mathbf{Ens}), \] one finds a simplicial set \(\mathrm{K}_\bullet = (\mathrm{K}_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}}\), with \(\mathrm{K}_i = \pi_0(S_i)\). Likewise, the \(S_{i\xi}\) (= \((i+1)\)-th fibred power of \(S'_\xi\) over \(\xi\)) form a simplicial object of \((\mathbf{Sch})_{/\xi}\), hence of \((\mathbf{Sch})_{/S}\), moreover equipped with a natural homomorphism of simplicial objects into \((S_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}}\), whence a simplicial set \(k_\bullet\) (with \(k_i = \pi_0(S_{i\xi})\)) and a canonical homomorphism \[ k_\bullet \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_\bullet. \] We can form a new simplicial set by taking the cone of this morphism (cf. 9.5.1): \[ \widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet = \mathrm{C\hat{o}ne}(k_\bullet \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_\bullet). \]

53 In this way, one obtains a "pointed simplicial set" \(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet\) (i.e. a simplicial set equipped with a homomorphism \(\widetilde{\xi} : e_\bullet \to \widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet\), where \(e_\bullet\) is the final simplicial set). We can construct its well-known combinatorial invariants \(\pi_0(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet,\widetilde{\xi})\) and \(\pi_1(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet,\widetilde{\xi})\), whose construction moreover involves only the components of degree \(\leqslant 1\) resp. of degree \(\leqslant 2\). These invariants are defined without restriction on S or \(S'\). One then verifies without difficulty, when the connected components of \(S_0\) and \(S_1\) are open and \(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet\) is connected,54 that \(\pi_1(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet,\widetilde{\xi})\) represents the functor \(G \mapsto \pi^1(S'/S,\xi;G)\), i.e. that we have: \[ \pi_1(S'/S;\xi) \simeq \pi_1(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet,\widetilde{\xi}). \] Let us also point out that when the morphism \(S' \to S\) is "universally submersive" (cf. SGA 1, IV 2.1), and the connected components of \(S'\) open, then55 the simplicial set \(\mathrm{K}_\bullet\), hence also \(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}_\bullet\), is connected.

blocks.title: Exemples
blocks.html:
Examples 6.4

It remains to give examples of enlarged fundamental groups. Let us take up again the examples of 1.6, that is, let k be an algebraically closed field, \(C_1\) a complete rational curve over k, having exactly one singular point, this point being an ordinary double point, and \(C_2\) a curve which is the union of two irreducible components, isomorphic to \(\mathbb{P}^1_k\) and meeting in exactly two points, which are ordinary double points of \(C_2\). In both cases, the enlarged fundamental group of the curve is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}\).56 In general, it would be worthwhile to take up again (simplifying and rectifying them) the results of SGA 1 IX 5, in the framework of the enlarged fundamental group. The examples of loc. cit., 5.5 would give as many examples of enlarged fundamental pro-groups that are not profinite. Thus, if instead of an ordinary double point, one took as first example a multiple point with n distinct branches,57 one would find as enlarged fundamental group the free (discrete!) group on \(n-1\) generators.

pages.title: 7. Classification of twisted constant preschemes and of groups of multiplicative type of finite type in terms of the enlarged fundamental group

blocks.html: 7. Classification of twisted constant preschemes and of groups of multiplicative type of finite type in terms of the enlarged fundamental group

Let S be a prescheme, which we again assume locally noetherian, to ensure that S and certain preschemes over S that we shall consider (in particular those étale over S, more generally those locally of finite type over S) are locally connected.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.0.1

Every twisted constant prescheme X over S which is locally trivial for the (fppf) topology (i.e. which is split by a faithfully flat morphism locally of finite presentation \(S' \to S\)) is quasi-isotrivial (i.e. one may even choose \(S' \to S\) étale surjective).

Indeed, we may assume S connected, hence X of type I, where I is a fixed set. Thus \(X' = X \times_S S'\) is isomorphic to \(I_{S'}\), so \(I_{S'}\) comes equipped with a descent datum relative to \(S' \to S\), i.e. we have an isomorphism \(I_{S''} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } I_{S''}\) satisfying the usual transitivity condition. Now, \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\) is locally noetherian hence locally connected, from which it follows that the automorphisms of \(I_{S''}\) correspond to the sections of \(G_{S''}\), where \(G = \mathrm{Aut}(I)\) is the group of permutations of I. In this way, one obtains a descent datum on \(G_{S'}\) (regarded as a trivial Galois principal bundle) relative to \(S' \to S\). By virtue of 5.4 this descent datum is effective, whence a Galois principal bundle P over S, with group G. By construction, it represents the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_S(I_S,X)\) in the category of preschemes over S that are locally noetherian. Consequently, the surjective étale base change \(P \to S\) splits X, so X is indeed quasi-isotrivial.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 7.0.2

One should take care that even if S is the spectrum of a field, it is not true in general that every twisted constant bundle over S is quasi-isotrivial. It suffices for example to take for X the scheme sum of a sequence of schemes of the form \(\mathrm{Spec}(k_i)\), where the \(k_i\) are separable extensions of k of strictly increasing degrees.

The proof given above shows at the same time that the classification of twisted constant bundles X over S, quasi-isotrivial and of type I, is equivalent to that of Galois principal bundles over S, with group \(G = \mathrm{Aut}(I)\). This is in fact an equivalence of categories. It can be put in a more convenient form as in SGA 1 V. For this, suppose S connected, and equipped with a geometric point \(\xi\). Consequently the enlarged fundamental pro-group \(\Pi = \Pi_1(S,\xi)\) is defined. On the other hand, for every quasi-isotrivial twisted constant bundle X over S, let \(\mathrm{I} = \mathrm{X}(\xi)\) be its set-theoretic fibre at \(\xi\). So X is of type I, and consequently associated as just said to a Galois principal bundle \(\mathrm{P} = \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_S(I_S,X)\) over S, with group \(G = \mathrm{Aut}(I)\). By the definition of \(\Pi\), one thus obtains a canonical homomorphism from \(\Pi\) to G, i.e. from one of the \(\Pi_i\) to G. As G is the group of permutations of \(\mathrm{I} = \mathrm{X}(\xi)\), this means that \(\Pi\) "operates continuously on \(\mathrm{I} = \mathrm{X}(\xi)\)", it being understood by this that the \(\pi_i\) (i large) operate on I, compatibly with the transition morphisms.

We leave it to the reader to verify that every S-morphism \(\mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) between quasi-isotrivial twisted constant bundles over S induces a map \(\mathrm{X}(\xi) \to \mathrm{Y}(\xi)\) compatible with the operations of \(\Pi\), and that the functor thus obtained is an equivalence of categories:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.0.3

Let S be a connected locally noetherian prescheme, \(\xi\) a geometric point of S, \(\Pi = \Pi_1(S,\xi)\) the enlarged fundamental pro-group of S at \(\xi\). Then the functor \[ \mathrm{X} \longmapsto \mathrm{X}(\xi) \] is an equivalence between the category of quasi-isotrivial twisted constant bundles over S and the category of sets on which \(\Pi\) operates continuously.

This functor is compatible with the operations of finite sums and of finite \(\varprojlim\). It follows for example that the quasi-isotrivial twisted constant groups (or rings etc.) over S correspond to the ordinary groups (resp. rings etc.) on which the pro-group \(\Pi\) operates continuously. In particular:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.0.4

The category of quasi-isotrivial twisted constant commutative groups over S is equivalent to the category of "\(\Pi\)-modules", i.e. of commutative groups M on which \(\Pi\) operates continuously.
Using now 5.7 one concludes from this:

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.1

Let S be a connected locally noetherian prescheme, \(\xi\) a geometric point of S, \(\Pi = \Pi_1(S,\xi)\) the enlarged fundamental pro-group of S at \(\xi\). Then the functor \[ \mathrm{G} \longmapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\kappa(\xi)\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{G}_\xi,\mathbb{G}_{m,\xi}) \] induces an anti-equivalence of the category of quasi-isotrivial groups of multiplicative type over S with the category of \(\Pi\)-modules.
Using 4.5 one concludes from this:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.2

The preceding functor induces an anti-equivalence of the category of groups of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, and the category of \(\Pi\)-modules that are of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\).

blocks.title: Exemple
blocks.html:
Example 7.3

Let us take again for S, for example, a complete rational curve over an algebraically closed field, having exactly one multiple point with \(n+1\) distinct branches. By 6.4, the enlarged fundamental group \(\Pi(S,\xi)\) is a free group on n generators. Hence, by 7.2, the classification of tori of relative dimension m over S is equivalent to the classification of systems of n endomorphisms \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) of the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module \(\mathbb{Z}^m\), up to an automorphism of \(\mathbb{Z}^m\). Except for \(n \leqslant 1\) or \(m \leqslant 1\), an explicit classification of such systems seems hopeless. One can at least define a host of nontrivial invariants for such a system, such as the characteristic polynomials of the \(A_i\).58

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 7.4

If one makes no hypothesis on S, it remains true that for a given ordinary commutative group M of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\), the category of groups of multiplicative type of type M over S is anti-equivalent to the category of Galois principal bundles over S, with group \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{Aut}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(\mathrm{M})\). This follows easily from 5.9 and 5.10.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 7.5

The theory of the fundamental pro-group that we have sketched in the present two sections will advantageously be written in the framework of general sites. In this form, it applies equally, for example, to ordinary topological spaces, and gives a satisfactory theory at least for a locally connected topological space (not necessarily locally simply connected). In this case also it seems that one cannot be content with defining a fundamental group, and that a pro-group is needed. Finally, let us remark that, once one has at one's disposal the language of topologies and of descent (which is really at the bottom of these questions), the exposition sketched here is also technically simpler than that of SGA 1 V, and should therefore in principle replace it.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: In particular, this shows that the tori of relative dimension 2 considered in 1.6 are not isotrivial.

pages.title: 8. Appendix: Elimination of certain affineness hypotheses

blocks.html: 8. Appendix: Elimination of certain affineness hypotheses
Our object is to prove the following generalization of 4.9.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.1

Let S be a prescheme, H an S-group prescheme flat and of finite presentation, with connected affine fibres.59 Let \(s \in S\), and suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a torus. Then there exists an open neighbourhood U of s such that \(\mathrm{H}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is a torus.
From this one concludes immediately:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.2

Let H be an S-group prescheme, flat and of finite presentation over S. For H to be a torus, it is necessary and sufficient that its fibres be tori.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 8.3

Even when S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, one cannot in 8.1 drop the hypothesis that the fibres of H (here the generic fibre) be affine, for there are examples of smooth groups over S whose generic fibre is an elliptic curve and whose special fibre is \(\mathbb{G}_m\).

Proof of 8.1. We may obviously assume \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\) affine, which brings us back by the standard procedure (cf. EGA IV2, 8.8.2) to the case where S is moreover noetherian. We begin by proving 8.2 in this case. By virtue of 4.9 we are reduced to proving that H is affine over S. We may therefore assume60 A noetherian local, and as the completion \(\widehat{\mathrm{A}}\) is faithfully flat over A, we are reduced by descent to the case where A is a complete noetherian local ring. Let \(\mathrm{S}'\) be the normalization of \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\); one knows by Nagata that it is finite over S (EGA 0IV, 23 1.5); moreover \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is surjective, hence \(\mathrm{H}' = \mathrm{H} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{H}\) is finite and surjective, so to prove that H is affine, it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{H}'\) is (EGA II, 6.7.1).

61 Replacing S by a connected component of \(\mathrm{S}'\), this reduces us to the case where A is a normal integral noetherian (semi-local) ring. Moreover, after replacing A by its normalization in a finite separable extension of its field of fractions, we may assume that the generic fibre \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) of H is diagonalizable, i.e. that we have an isomorphism \[ u_\eta : \mathrm{H}_\eta \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{T}_\eta, \] where \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}^r_{m,\mathrm{S}}\). Now we have the

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.4

Let S be a locally noetherian, normal and irreducible prescheme, with generic point \(\eta\), H a group prescheme over S smooth and with connected fibres, T a group prescheme of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, \(u_\eta : \mathrm{H}_\eta \to \mathrm{T}_\eta\) a homomorphism of algebraic groups over \(\kappa(\eta)\). Then \(u_\eta\) extends to a group homomorphism \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{T}\).

After replacing S by its normalization in a finite separable extension of its field of functions, we may assume T diagonalizable (which is moreover the case in the application we have in view). Then T is a closed subgroup of a group of the form \(\mathbb{G}^r_{m,\mathrm{S}}\), which reduces us to the case where \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\). Everything comes down to proving that \(u_\eta\), regarded as a rational map from H to \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\), is everywhere defined (for the morphism \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{T}\) extending it is then necessarily a group homomorphism). We may regard \(u_\eta\) as an invertible section f of the structure sheaf of \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\), and we must show that it extends to an invertible section of the structure sheaf of H. Now H, being smooth over S normal, is normal (SGA 1, II 3.1), so it suffices to find a closed part Z of H of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) such that f extends to an invertible section of the structure sheaf of \(\mathrm{H} - \mathrm{Z}\). This reduces us at once to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A (by localizing at the points of codimension 1 of S). Let t be a uniformizer of A, \(t'\) the section of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{H}}\) it defines, so that the special fibre \(\mathrm{H}_0\) equals \(\mathrm{V}(t')\). By hypothesis \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is smooth over the residue field k, and connected. Then f is a rational function on H having neither zeros nor poles in \(\mathrm{H} - \mathrm{H}_0\); as \(\mathrm{H}_0 = \mathrm{V}(t')\) is an irreducible divisor, there exists an integer \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\) such that \(t'^n f = t^n f\) has neither zeros nor poles, i.e. is an invertible section of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{H}}\). It therefore defines a morphism \(v : \mathrm{H} \to \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\), and as \(v_\eta = t'^n u_\eta\) and \(u_\eta\) is a group homomorphism, v transforms the unit section of H into a section of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) whose value at the generic point of S is \(t^n\); as this is a section of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\), \(t^n\) must be a unit, i.e. \(n=0\), so v extends \(u_\eta\), which completes the proof of 8.4.

Applying this lemma to the present case, one finds a group homomorphism \[ u : \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}^r_{m,\mathrm{S}} \] which induces an isomorphism on the generic fibres. Let us prove that u is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.5

For every integer \(n > 0\) prime to the residue characteristic of A, \({}_n\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{Ker}(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{H}})\) is finite over S.

If n is prime to the residue characteristic of A, it is prime to all the residue characteristics of the points of S. Hence \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{H}}\) induces an étale morphism on every fibre of H, consequently \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{H}}\) is étale (SGA 1, I 5.9), so its kernel \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is étale over S. On the other hand, \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is separated over S since H is(*)62. Moreover, all its fibres have the same rank \(n^r\), since the fibres of H are tori, all of the same dimension r (H being smooth over S). One concludes that \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is finite over S (SGA 1, I 10.9 or EGA IV4, 18.2.9).

Hence, by the preceding lemma, \(u({}_n\mathrm{H})\) is a closed part of T. As on the generic fibre \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\) this part is identical to \({}_n(\mathrm{T}_\eta)\), it follows that it contains the closure of this part, namely \({}_n\mathrm{T}\). Now for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), the \({}_n(\mathrm{T}_s)\) are dense in \(\mathrm{T}_s\); as \(u_s(\mathrm{H}_s)\) is a closed part (VIB 1.4.2) containing them, one sees that \(u_s(\mathrm{H}_s) = \mathrm{T}_s\), so u is surjective. As a surjective homomorphism of tori of the same dimension over a field is flat,63 it follows that u induces a flat morphism on each fibre, hence u is flat (SGA 1 I 5.9). Consequently, \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{Ker}(u)\) is flat over S,64 hence equal to the closure of its generic fibre \(\mathrm{K}_\eta\). Now \(\mathrm{K}_\eta\) is the unit group by construction, and as K is separated over S (since H is), its unit section is closed, from which it follows that K is the unit group. So u is a monomorphism; as we have seen that it is faithfully flat, it is therefore an isomorphism (cf. SGA 1, I 5.1 or EGA IV4, 17.9.1). This proves that H is a torus, and hence completes the proof of 8.2.65

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 8.5.1

Instead of invoking 8.5 one may also invoke Zariski's "Main Theorem", which implies directly that u is an open immersion, hence an isomorphism.66

To prove 8.1, we are reduced, thanks to the quasi-isotriviality theorem, to the case where S is local, s its closed point,67 and to proving that under the other hypotheses made, H is then a torus. By virtue of 8.2 already proved, we are reduced to proving that the fibres of H are tori. We may assume S is the spectrum of a complete noetherian local ring A. By virtue of 3.3 there exist for every \(n > 0\) a group of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{T}_n\) finite over S, and an isomorphism \((\mathrm{T}_n)_s \simeq {}_n(\mathrm{H}_s)\), where s is the closed point of S. Proceeding as in 3.1 and using the fact that \(\mathrm{T}_n\) is finite over S,68 one sees that the preceding isomorphism comes from a homomorphism \(u_n : \mathrm{T}_n \to \mathrm{H}\), moreover uniquely determined. (Pass to the limit over the artinian quotients of A). Moreover, by virtue of the uniqueness properties, \(u_n : \mathrm{T}_n \to \mathrm{H}\) is deduced from \(u_m : \mathrm{T}_m \to \mathrm{H}\) by restriction to \({}_n(\mathrm{T}_m) \simeq \mathrm{T}_n\) when m is a multiple of n. It follows from IX 6.6 that the \(u_n\) are monomorphisms, so the \(\mathrm{T}_n\) are subgroups of H, and for m a multiple of n, we have \(\mathrm{T}_n = {}_n(\mathrm{T}_m)\). Hence for every \(t \in \mathrm{S}\), the \((\mathrm{T}_n)_t\) are subgroups of \(\mathrm{H}_t\), of type \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\) (where \(r = \dim \mathrm{H}_s = \dim \mathrm{H}_t\)), such that for m a multiple of n, we have \((\mathrm{T}_n)_t = {}_n(\mathrm{T}_m)_t\). The fact that \(\mathrm{H}_t\) is a torus now follows from the

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.6

Let H be a smooth affine algebraic group over an algebraically closed field k, \((\mathrm{T}_n)_{n>0}\) a family of subgroups of multiplicative type of H, such that for every integer \(n > 0\), \(\mathrm{T}_n\) is of type \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\), and for every multiple m of n, we have \(\mathrm{T}_n = {}_n(\mathrm{T}_m)\). Under these conditions, H contains a torus of dimension \(\geqslant r\) containing the \(\mathrm{T}_n\); hence if H is connected of dimension \(\leqslant r\), H is a torus of dimension r.

This is an exercise in affine algebraic groups, which we shall treat by dint of references to Bible. We restrict ourselves to considering the \(\mathrm{T}_n\) for n prime to the characteristic. Let K be the closure of the union of the \(\mathrm{T}_n\) in H, equipped with the induced reduced structure; then standard reasoning shows that K is a commutative algebraic subgroup of H. By virtue of Bible, § 4.5, th. 4, K is therefore isomorphic to a product \(\mathrm{K}_u \times \mathrm{K}_s\), with \(\mathrm{K}_u\) "unipotent" and \(\mathrm{K}_s\) diagonalizable. Every diagonalizable subgroup of K is contained in \(\mathrm{K}_s\), so the \(\mathrm{T}_n\) are subgroups of \(\mathrm{K}_s\), hence \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{K}_s\). Write \(\mathrm{K} = \mathbf{D}(\mathrm{M})\), with M an ordinary commutative group of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\); then \(\mathrm{T}_n \subseteq \mathrm{K}\) means that M admits a quotient group isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\). This being true for every integer n prime to the characteristic of k (the powers of a fixed prime number would suffice), it follows that M is of rank \(\geqslant r\), so K contains a torus of dimension r, say T. When H is connected of dimension r, it follows that \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{H}\), which completes the proof of 8.6. Thus 8.1 is proved.

blocks.title: Remarques
blocks.html:
Remarks 8.7

Using 8.1, it should not be difficult to give analogous generalizations of 4.7 and 4.8. A more interesting study would be that of the situation of 8.1 in which one drops the hypothesis that the fibres of H be affine. One can show that there then exists an open neighbourhood U of s such that \(\mathrm{H}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is commutative and such that for every \(t \in \mathrm{U}\), the geometric fibre \(\mathrm{H}_{\overline{t}}\) is an extension of an abelian variety by a torus.69 Of course, in questions of this kind, one may restrict to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, s its closed point, t its generic point. One can generalize this result in the following way. For every connected smooth algebraic group G over an algebraically closed field k, a well-known theorem of Chevalley tells us that G is (in a unique way) an extension of an abelian variety A by a smooth connected affine group V. Let us call the abelian rank (resp. reductive rank, resp. nilpotent rank, resp. semisimple dimension) of G, and denote by \(\rho_{\mathrm{ab}}(\mathrm{G})\) (resp. \(\rho_r(\mathrm{G})\), resp. \(\rho_n(\mathrm{G})\), resp. \(d_s(\mathrm{G})\)), the dimension of A, resp. the dimension of the maximal tori of G, resp. the dimension of the Cartan subgroups70 of G, resp. the dimension of the quotient of G (or equally of V) by its radical (cf. Bible for all these notions). Let us also introduce the unipotent rank \(\rho_u(\mathrm{G}) = \rho_u(\mathrm{V}) = \rho_n(\mathrm{G}) - \rho_r(\mathrm{G}) - \rho_{\mathrm{ab}}(\mathrm{G})\). When G is not algebraically closed, we designate by the same names and the same notation the corresponding invariants for \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{k}}\), where \(\overline{k}\) is the algebraic closure of k. This said, let G be a group scheme smooth over the spectrum S of a discrete valuation ring, and let \(\rho_{\mathrm{ab}}\) etc. (resp. \(\rho'_{\mathrm{ab}}\) etc.) be the invariants associated with the special fibre (resp. with the generic fibre); then one has the inequalities: \[ \left\{ \begin{aligned} \rho_{\mathrm{ab}} &\leqslant \rho'_{\mathrm{ab}} \\ \rho_r + \rho_{\mathrm{ab}} &\leqslant \rho'_r + \rho'_{\mathrm{ab}} \\ d_s &\leqslant d'_s \end{aligned} \right. \qquad \left\{ \begin{aligned} \rho_n &\geqslant \rho'_n \\ \rho_u &\geqslant \rho'_u. \end{aligned} \right. \] It amounts to the same to say that if G is smooth of finite type over an arbitrary base S, the functions \(s \mapsto \rho_{\mathrm{ab}}(s)\), \(\rho_{\mathrm{ab}}(s) + \rho_r(s)\), \(d_s(s)\) are lower semicontinuous, and the functions \(s \mapsto \rho_n(s)\), \(\rho_u(s)\) are upper semicontinuous.71 The same results probably still hold without assuming G smooth over S, but merely flat of finite presentation over S, agreeing to denote, for an algebraic group G over an algebraically closed field k, by \(\rho_{\mathrm{ab}}(\mathrm{G})\) etc. the corresponding invariants of \(\mathrm{G}^0_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\). In this Seminar, we present some results of this type for G affine over S, or more generally with affine fibres: in this case, we shall verify the semicontinuity properties for \(\rho_r\), \(\rho_n\), hence for \(\rho_u = \rho_n - \rho_r\), and the continuity of \(\rho_s\) in a neighbourhood of a point s whose fibre is a reductive group.71
One can generalize 8.2 when G is already assumed commutative, in the following way:

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.8

72 Let G be a commutative S-group prescheme, flat and of finite presentation over S, with connected affine fibres. Let \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), and suppose that

  1. a)

    if \(\overline{k}\) denotes an algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\), \((\mathrm{G}_{\overline{k}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a torus.

  2. b)

    There exists a generization t of s such that \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is smooth over \(\kappa(t)\).

Under these conditions, there exists an open neighbourhood U of s such that \(\mathrm{G}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is a torus. (Note moreover that if one assumes only that for every generization t of s, \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is affine resp. connected, one easily deduces that the same conclusion holds for t in an open neighbourhood of s).

Proof of 8.8. It suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\). Indeed, as G is flat of finite presentation over S, it then follows that G is smooth over S above a neighbourhood of s (cf. 3.5), but then we are under the conditions of 8.1. To prove that \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\), the usual procedure reduces us to the case where S is affine noetherian. Choosing a homomorphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) of a spectrum of a discrete valuation ring \(\mathrm{S}'\) into S, sending the closed point to s and the generic point \(\eta\) to t, we are reduced to the case where S is itself the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, which we may moreover assume complete with algebraically closed residue field, and where s and \(t = \eta\) are respectively the closed point and the generic point of S. So G is flat, separated, of finite type over S, the generic fibre \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) is smooth and connected, and the special fibre \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is such that \(\mathrm{T}_0 = (\mathrm{G}_0)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a torus. Let m be an integer \(> 0\) prime to the residue characteristic at s, hence also to that at \(\eta\); one then knows that \(m \cdot \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{G}}\) is an étale morphism fibre by fibre (VIIA 8.4), hence an étale morphism since G is flat over S (SGA 1 I 5.9), so its kernel \({}_m\mathrm{G}\) is étale over S, and as G is separated(*)73 of finite type over S, so is \({}_m\mathrm{G}\). As \(({}_m\mathrm{G})_0 = {}_m(\mathrm{T}_0)\), its degree is \(m^r\), where \(r = \dim \mathrm{T}_0 = \dim \mathrm{G}_0 = \dim \mathrm{G}\). It follows that the rank of \(({}_m\mathrm{G})_\eta = {}_m(\mathrm{G}_\eta)\) is \(\geqslant m^r\), which already proves (using 8.6) that \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) is a torus of dimension r, since the two fibres of \({}_m\mathrm{G}\) have the same rank, hence as in 8.5 that \({}_m\mathrm{G}\) is finite over S.74

Note that since S is complete with algebraically closed residue field, the finite étale covering \({}_m\mathrm{G}\) decomposes completely, so through every point of \({}_m\mathrm{G}_0\) there passes a section of G over S; in particular the set of points of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) through which passes a section of G over S is everywhere dense. Now we have the following:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.9

Let S be a locally noetherian regular prescheme of dimension 1, G an S-group prescheme flat and locally of finite type, such that \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) is smooth over \(\kappa(\eta)\) for every maximal point \(\eta\) of S (which implies that G is reduced). Suppose that the normalized scheme X of G is finite over G (this is the case, by Nagata, if S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring, cf. EGA IV2, 7.7.4), and let \(\mathrm{G}'\) be the open set of X formed by the points at which X is smooth over S. With this notation:

  1. a)

    If the projection \(\mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is surjective, then there exists on \(\mathrm{G}'\) a unique structure of S-group prescheme such that the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G}\) is a group homomorphism.

  2. b)

    Suppose that for every closed point s of S, the set of points of \(\mathrm{G}^0_s\) through which passes an étale quasi-section is dense in \(\mathrm{G}^0_s\) for the Zariski topology. Then X is regular, we are under the conditions of a), and the map \(\Gamma(\mathrm{G}'/\mathrm{S}) \to \Gamma(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) is bijective.

In the case of interest to us, this lemma applies and gives us a group homomorphism \(u : \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G}\), where \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth over S, and where \(u_\eta\) is an isomorphism \(\mathrm{G}'_\eta \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{G}_\eta\). After replacing \(\mathrm{G}'\) by an open subgroup, we may assume that \(\mathrm{G}'_0\) is connected, and as \(u_0 : \mathrm{G}'_0 \to \mathrm{G}_0\) induces a surjective morphism \(\mathrm{G}'_0 \to \mathrm{T}_0\), where \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is a torus of the same dimension as \(\mathrm{G}'_0\), one easily concludes that \(\mathrm{G}'_0\) is a torus (for example by using 8.6, or by referring to Bible, § 7.3, th. 3 a)). Consequently, by virtue of 8.2, \(\mathrm{G}'\) is a torus, but then by virtue of IX 6.8, \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}'\), and as its generic fibre is reduced to the unit group, it is the unit group, so u is a monomorphism. Using now VIII 7.9 it follows that u is an immersion. Being surjective, and G being reduced, it follows that u is an isomorphism, which completes the proof of 8.8. It remains to prove 8.9. To prove a), note that the uniqueness of the group law on \(\mathrm{G}'\) making \(u : \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G}\) a group homomorphism is clear, since one knows the group law of \(\mathrm{G}'\) on the generic fibre (assuming S irreducible, which is permissible). For the existence, one easily reduces to the case where S is local, the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, and thanks to the uniqueness, and to the fact that the operation of integral closure commutes with an étale extension of the base, one may perform étale extensions of the base on S, which reduces us to the case where A is "strictly local", i.e. henselian with separably closed residue field. The same reduction applies for b), but under the hypothesis made in b), one may now replace "étale quasi-section" by "section". Note that \(\mathrm{G}'\) being smooth over S and X normal, \(\mathrm{G}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}\) is normal (being smooth over X which is normal), so the composed morphism \(\mathrm{G}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) factors as \[ p : \mathrm{G}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}. \] Let us prove that this last morphism induces on the open set \(\mathrm{G}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}\) a morphism \[ \mathrm{G}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{G}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'. \] We must therefore show that \(\mathrm{G}'_0 \times_k \mathrm{G}'_0\) is mapped into the open set \(\mathrm{G}'_0\) of \(\mathrm{X}_0\); it suffices to see that for every point \(g'_0\) of \(\mathrm{G}'_0\) with values in k, the morphism \[ \text{(x)} \qquad h'_0 \longmapsto p_0(g'_0, h'_0) \] from \(\mathrm{G}'_0\) to \(\mathrm{X}_0\) takes its values in \(\mathrm{G}'_0\). Now as \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth over S and S is henselian, every \(g'_0\) as above is induced by a section \(g'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\) over S, and one sees at once that the morphism (x) above is then induced by the morphism \(h' \mapsto p(g', h')\) from \(\mathrm{G}'\) to X, itself deduced by transport of structure from the automorphism \(h \mapsto g \cdot h\) of G, left translation by the section g of G which is the image of \(g'\). Hence \(h' \mapsto p(g', h')\) is itself an automorphism of X, so it maps \(\mathrm{G}'\) into \(\mathrm{G}'\), which proves our assertion. It remains to prove that the composition law thus obtained on \(\mathrm{G}'\) is a group law. Associativity follows at once from the associativity of the generic fibre (isomorphic to that of G). On the other hand, the symmetry automorphism of the S-prescheme G induces an automorphism of X, which therefore leaves \(\mathrm{G}'\) stable and induces an automorphism \(\sigma\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\). One then sees that the latter has the properties of an inverse for the composition law on \(\mathrm{G}'\), for this again comes down to verifying the commutativity of certain diagrams involving fibred powers of \(\mathrm{G}'\) over S, and these being smooth over S, it suffices to verify their commutativity on the generic fibre, which is clear. This proves part a) of 8.9. Let us prove b). Let \(\mathrm{Z}'\) be the set of \(x \in \mathrm{X}\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{X},x}\) is non-regular; it is a closed part by virtue of a theorem of Nagata (EGA IV2, 6.12.6), obviously contained in \(\mathrm{X}_0\); let Z be its image in G, which is therefore a closed part of \(\mathrm{G}_0\). Then Z is a nowhere dense part of \(\mathrm{G}_0\), i.e. contains no maximal point y of \(\mathrm{G}_0\). Indeed, as \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is defined in G by an equation \(t = 0\) (where t is a uniformizer of the discrete valuation ring A), \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G},y}\) is of dimension 1 by the Hauptidealsatz, so for every x of X above y, \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{X},x}\) is of dimension 1, hence a discrete valuation ring since X is normal, hence regular in codimension 1. On the other hand, it is obvious that for every section g of G over S, \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is stable under the automorphism of X defined by transport of structure from the left translation by g in G, so Z is stable under the left translation in \(\mathrm{G}_0\) defined by \(g_0\). Now by hypothesis the set of these \(g_0\) is dense in \(\mathrm{G}^0_0\). As Z is stable under translation by these \(g_0\), and is a nowhere dense closed set, it follows at once that \(\mathrm{Z} = \varnothing\), whence \(\mathrm{Z}' = \varnothing\), so X is regular. Consequently, X is smooth over S at every point through which a section passes. Now every section of G over S lifts in a unique way to a section of X over S, hence of \(\mathrm{G}'\) over S. This is so in particular for the unit section, which proves that the image of \(\mathrm{G}'\) in S is S, i.e. that we are under the conditions of (a). This completes the proof of 8.9 and hence of 8.8.

blocks.html: 9. Addenda
75
9.1. Simplicial sets, topoi, groupoids, and topological spaces.

blocks.title: Notations
blocks.html:
Notation 9.1.1

(1) Let E be a simplicial set. One can associate to it the following objects:

(2) a topos \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{E}^\sim\), obtained from the topoi naturally associated with the sets \(\mathrm{E}_i\), following the procedure described in [Del74], 6.3.1 (see also [Ill72], VI.5.2 and SGA 4 VI.7);

(3) a groupoid \(\mathrm{G} = \Pi(\mathrm{E})\), whose objects are the elements of \(\mathrm{E}_0\) (the "vertices") and whose arrows are defined in [GZ67], II.7;

(4) a topological space \(\mathrm{X} = |\mathrm{E}|\) (a cell complex), called the "geometric" (or "topological") "realization" (cf. loc. cit., III.1).
Let us remark that a sheaf on T is nothing other than a simplicial set over E.
9.2. Locally constant sheaves; descent data.

blocks.title: Définitions
blocks.html:
Definitions 9.2.1

One calls a locally constant sheaf on:

(1) a simplicial set E, any morphism of simplicial sets \(\mathrm{E}' \to \mathrm{E}\) such that for every \(i \in \mathbb{N}\) and every \(e \in \mathrm{E}_i\), the face operators d induce isomorphisms \(\mathrm{E}'_e \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{E}'_{d(e)}\) between the fibres (cf. [AM69], § 10);

(2) a topos T, any object F of T such that there exist an epimorphism \(\mathrm{U} \to 1\) and an isomorphism \(\mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{U} \simeq f^*\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{U}\), where I is a set and \(f : \mathrm{T} \to \mathbf{Ens}\) is the final morphism (cf. SGA 4, IX.2);

(3) a groupoid G, any presheaf on G, that is, any contravariant functor from G to the category of sets (cf. [GZ67], append. I.1.2);

(4) a topological space X, any sheaf of sets on X, locally constant in the usual sense.

Finally:

(5) one calls a descent datum on a simplicial set E the datum of a sheaf F on \(\mathrm{E}^\sim_0\) (that is, a set-valued function on \(\mathrm{E}_0\)) and of an isomorphism \(\alpha : p_1^*\mathrm{F} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } p_2^*\mathrm{F}\), where \(p_1,p_2 : \mathrm{E}^\sim_1 \to \mathrm{E}^\sim_0\) are the morphisms (deduced from the) faces, satisfying the usual cocycle relation (cf. Exp. IV, 2.1(1) and infra).
The morphisms between these five types of objects, as well as the associated inverse image functors, are defined in the obvious way.
9.3. Some equivalences of categories.
Let E be a simplicial set and respectively T, G and X the associated topos, groupoid and topological space.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.3.1

The categories of locally constant sheaves on E, T, G, X, as well as the category of descent data on E, are equivalent.
Sketch of proof. — Denote by (1) to (5) the categories of objects defined in the preceding paragraph.

• (1)⇔(5). This is a particular case of [AM69], 10.6 (see also [GZ67], append. I.2.3, [Fri82], ?.5.6 or [Ill72], VI.8.1.6). An equivalence of categories is given by the functor associating to the object \((\mathrm{E}' \to \mathrm{E})\) the pair \((\mathrm{E}'_0,\alpha)\) where \(\mathrm{E}'_0\) is regarded as a sheaf on \(\mathrm{E}_0\) and where \(\alpha\) is the unique isomorphism \(p_1^*\mathrm{E}'_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } p_2^*\mathrm{E}'_0\) whose fibre at each \(y \in \mathrm{E}_1\) — with images denoted \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) under the two projections — is given by the isomorphisms \((\mathrm{E}'_0)_{x_1} \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathrm{E}'_1)_y \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathrm{E}'_0)_{x_2}\).
  • (5)⇔(3). Obvious: one of the two relations defining the morphisms in the groupoid G associated with E is a cocycle relation.
  • (1)⇔(4). Cf. [GZ67], append. I.3.2.1.
  • (1)⇔(2). One must show that an object over E is a locally constant sheaf in the simplicial sense if and only if it is one as a sheaf on \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{E}^\sim\). This follows from the fact that the locally constant sheaves \(\mathrm{F}_\bullet\) on T are none other than the cartesian sheaves, that is, those for which the arrows \(([n] \to [m])^*\mathrm{F}_m \to \mathrm{F}_n\) are isomorphisms. This last point is a particular case of a general fact about simplicial topoi combined with the fact that every sheaf on \(\mathrm{E}^\sim_0\) is locally constant. (See also [Ill72], VI.8.1.6.)
Q.E.D.

For every group H, the equivalences of categories above induce equivalences between the categories of H-torsors, the latter being locally constant sheaves equipped with an action of H, with fibres isomorphic to H acting on itself by translation.
9.4. Fundamental groups and groupoids.

It follows from the preceding equivalences of categories that for every group H and every simplicial set E, the sets of isomorphism classes of H-torsors \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{E},\mathrm{H})\), \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{E}^\sim,\mathrm{H})\), \(\pi_0(\mathrm{Hom}(\Pi(\mathrm{E}),\mathrm{BH}))\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1(|\mathrm{E}|,\mathrm{H})\) are naturally in bijection. Recall that BH denotes the one-point groupoid associated with H and \(\pi_0\) the functor associating to a category the set of isomorphism classes of its objects. Likewise, if e is a point of E, the preceding equivalences induce bijections between the sets of isomorphism classes of H-torsors trivialized over e, denoted respectively \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{E}\ \mathrm{rel}\ e,\mathrm{H})\), \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{E}^\sim\ \mathrm{rel}\ e^\sim,\mathrm{H})\), \(\mathrm{Hom}(\pi_1(\Pi(\mathrm{E}),e),\mathrm{H})\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1(|\mathrm{E}|\ \mathrm{rel}\ |e|,\mathrm{H})\). Recall that \(\pi_1(\Pi(\mathrm{E}),e)\) denotes the group \(\mathrm{Isom}_{\Pi(\mathrm{E})}(e,e)\). For varying H, these functors are represented, in the connected case, by a group denoted \(\pi_1(\mathrm{E},e)\). The group \(\pi_1(\mathrm{E},e)\) is isomorphic to \(\pi_1(\Pi(\mathrm{E}),e)\) and \(\pi_1(|\mathrm{E}|,|e|)\), and thus also to the fundamental group of a simplicial set as defined by Kan (cf. e.g. [May67], 16.1 or [Ill72], I.2.1.1). (Recall that the set \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{E},\mathrm{H})\) is for its part isomorphic to the set \(\mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1(\mathrm{E},e),\mathrm{H})\) of morphisms to H modulo conjugation, also denoted \(\mathrm{Hom}\,\mathrm{ext}(\pi_1(\mathrm{E},e),\mathrm{H})\).)

blocks.title: Définitions
blocks.html:
Definitions 9.5.1

(1) Let \(f : \mathrm{E}' \to \mathrm{E}\) be a morphism of simplicial sets. Recall (cf. for example [Del74], 6.3.1) that one denotes by \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) the pointed simplicial set whose underlying set in degree \(n \geqslant 0\) is \[ \mathrm{E}_n \amalg \left(\coprod_{i<n} \mathrm{E}'_i\right) \amalg \star, \] where \(\star\) is a singleton. We leave it to the reader to define the simplicial maps, the definition of the faces in rank at most two being recalled below. (See also [GZ67], VI.2 for a pointed variant.) The category of locally constant sheaves on \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is equivalent to the category of locally constant sheaves on E equipped with a trivialization of the inverse image on \(\mathrm{E}'\). The simplicial set \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is naturally pointed by \(\star \to \mathrm{C}(f)\).

(2) Let \(f : \mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{T}\) be a morphism of topoi. Denote by \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) the topos whose objects are the quintuples \((\mathrm{F},\mathrm{F}',\mathrm{A},\alpha : f^*\mathrm{F} \to \mathrm{F}', \beta : \mathrm{A} \to \Gamma(\mathrm{T}',\mathrm{F}'))\), where F (resp. \(\mathrm{F}'\)) is an object of T (resp. \(\mathrm{T}'\)), A is a set, and \(\alpha\) (resp. \(\beta\)) is a morphism in \(\mathrm{T}'\) (resp. \(\mathbf{Ens}\)). (See also [Del80], 4.3.4 and [Ill72], III.4 for a variant of this construction.) The category of locally constant sheaves on \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is equivalent to the category of locally constant sheaves on T equipped with a trivialization of the inverse image on \(\mathrm{T}'\). The topos \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is naturally pointed by the fibre functor sending the quintuple \((\mathrm{F},\mathrm{F}',\mathrm{A},\alpha : f^*\mathrm{F} \to \mathrm{F}', \beta : \mathrm{A} \to \Gamma(\mathrm{T}',\mathrm{F}'))\) to the set A.

(3) Let \(f : \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G}\) be a morphism of groupoids. Denote by \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) the colimit of the diagram \[ \mathrm{G} \longleftarrow \mathrm{G}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}1 \] where B1 is the one-point category (one object, one arrow). The category of locally constant sheaves on \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is equivalent to the category of locally constant sheaves on G equipped with a trivialization of the inverse image on \(\mathrm{G}'\).

(4) Let \(f : \mathrm{X}' \to \mathrm{X}\) be a morphism of topological spaces. Denote by \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) the colimit of the diagram \[ \star \longleftarrow \mathrm{X}' \times \{0\} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}' \times [0,1] \longleftarrow \mathrm{X}' \times \{1\} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}. \] The category of locally constant sheaves on \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is equivalent to the category of locally constant sheaves on X equipped with a trivialization of the inverse image on \(\mathrm{X}'\). The topological space \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) is naturally pointed by \(\star \to \mathrm{C}(f)\).
9.5.2. Descent data on the cone of a simplicial map.

Let \(f : \mathrm{E}' \to \mathrm{E}\) be a morphism of simplicial sets and \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) its cone (cf. supra, (1)). Let us use the letter p (resp. q, r) to denote the face maps of \(\mathrm{E}'\) (resp. E, \(\mathrm{C}(f)\)). Before stating the proposition below, let us make explicit the faces r in degree at most two in terms of p and q. By convention, \(p_{ij}\) (resp. \(p_i\)) is the face map \(\mathrm{E}'_2 = \mathrm{E}'_{\{1,2,3\}} \to \mathrm{E}'_1 = \mathrm{E}'_{\{1,2\}}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{E}'_1 = \mathrm{E}'_{\{1,2\}} \to \mathrm{E}'_0 = \mathrm{E}'_{\{1\}}\)) corresponding to the increasing map \(\{1,2\} \to \{1,2,3\}\) (resp. \(\{1\} \to \{1,2\}\)) with image \(\{i,j\}\) (resp. \(\{i\}\)).(2) We adopt the same convention for the faces of E and \(\mathrm{C}(f)\). The morphism \[ r_1 : \mathrm{C}(f)_1 = \mathrm{E}_1 \amalg \mathrm{E}'_0 \amalg \star \to \mathrm{C}(f)_0 = \mathrm{E}_0 \amalg \star \] (resp. \(r_2\)) is:

• \(q_1 : \mathrm{E}_1 \to \mathrm{E}_0\) (resp. \(q_2 : \mathrm{E}_1 \to \mathrm{E}_0\)) on \(\mathrm{E}_1\);
  • \(\mathrm{E}'_0 \to \star\) (resp. \(f_0 : \mathrm{E}'_0 \to \mathrm{E}_0\)) on \(\mathrm{E}'_0\);
  • \(\star \to \star\) on \(\star\).

Likewise the morphism \[ r_{21} : \mathrm{C}(f)_2 = \mathrm{E}_2 \amalg \mathrm{E}'_1 \amalg \mathrm{E}'_0 \amalg \star \to \mathrm{C}(f)_1 = \mathrm{E}_1 \amalg \mathrm{E}'_0 \amalg \star \] (resp. \(r_{32}\), \(r_{31}\)) is:

• \(q_{21} : \mathrm{E}_2 \to \mathrm{E}_1\) (resp. \(q_{32}\), \(q_{31}\)) on \(\mathrm{E}_2\);
  • \(p_1 : \mathrm{E}'_1 \to \mathrm{E}'_0\) (resp. \(f_1 : \mathrm{E}'_1 \to \mathrm{E}_1\), \(p_2 : \mathrm{E}'_1 \to \mathrm{E}'_0\)) on \(\mathrm{E}'_1\);
  • \(\mathrm{E}'_0 \to \star\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Id} : \mathrm{E}'_0 \to \mathrm{E}'_0\), \(\mathrm{Id} : \mathrm{E}'_0 \to \mathrm{E}'_0\)) on \(\mathrm{E}'_0\).

Let H be a descent datum on \(\mathrm{C}(f)\), that is, a sheaf \(\mathrm{H}_0\) on \(\mathrm{C}(f)_0 = \mathrm{E}_0 \amalg \star\) equipped with an isomorphism \(\gamma : r_1^*\mathrm{H}_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } r_2^*\mathrm{H}_0\) between its inverse images on \(\mathrm{C}(f)_1 = \mathrm{E}_1 \amalg \mathrm{E}'_0 \amalg \star\) satisfying the cocycle relation \(r_{31}^*\gamma = r_{32}^*\gamma \circ r_{21}^*\gamma\). The restriction of the isomorphism \(\gamma\) to \(\mathrm{E}_1\) (resp. \(\mathrm{E}'_0\)) is a descent datum \(\beta : q_1^*\mathrm{G}_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } q_2^*\mathrm{G}_0\) where \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is the restriction of \(\mathrm{H}_0\) to \(\mathrm{E}_0\) (resp. a trivialization \(t : \mathrm{H}_0(\star) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f^*\mathrm{G}_0 =: \mathrm{F}_0\) where \(\mathrm{H}_0(\star)\) is the constant sheaf with stalk \(\mathrm{H}_0(\star)\)). The restriction of the cocycle relation satisfied by \(\gamma\) to \(\mathrm{E}_2\) (resp. \(\mathrm{E}'_1\)) is the cocycle relation for \(\beta\) (resp. \(p_2^*(t) = f_1^*(\beta) \circ p_1^*(t)\)). This last relation means that the trivialization t is compatible with the descent datum \(f_1^*(\beta)\) induced by \(\beta\) on \(\mathrm{F}_0\). It follows:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.5.3

Let \(f : \mathrm{E}' \to \mathrm{E}\) be a morphism of simplicial sets and \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) its cone, pointed by \(\star\). The category of descent data on \(\mathrm{C}(f)\) trivialized over \(\star\) is equivalent to the category of descent data on E equipped with a trivialization of the induced descent datum on \(\mathrm{E}'\).
9.6. Representability of the functor \(\pi^1(\mathrm{S}'/\mathrm{S},\xi;-)\).

The notation is that of page 90, and from now on the connected components of \(\mathrm{S}_0 = \mathrm{S}'\) and \(\mathrm{S}_1 = \mathrm{S}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\) are assumed open. Under this hypothesis, one has the following explicit combinatorial description, which follows immediately from the reminders above.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.6.1

For every group G, the set \(\pi^1(\mathrm{S}'/\mathrm{S},\xi;\mathrm{G})\) of isomorphism classes of descent data equipped with a trivialization above \(\xi\) is in natural bijection with the relative non-abelian cohomology set \[ \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{K}\ \mathrm{rel}\ k, \mathrm{G}) := \mathrm{H}^1(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}}\ \mathrm{rel}\ \star, \mathrm{G}). \]

It follows that this functor is representable by a group, denoted \(\pi_1(\mathrm{K},k)\) (the "relative fundamental group"), as soon as the simplicial set \(\widetilde{\mathrm{K}} = \mathrm{C}(k \to \mathrm{K})\) is connected. One verifies immediately that this is the case if and only if the map \(\pi_0(k) \to \pi_0(\mathrm{K})\) is surjective. It suffices in particular that the simplicial set K be connected. As indicated in the text, this is the case if the scheme S is connected and the morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is universally submersive.
9.7. Contractibility of k, counterexample to the injectivity of \(k_\bullet \to \mathrm{K}_\bullet\).
The two following propositions make N.D.E. (53) precise.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.7.1

Let \(\kappa\) be an algebraically closed field, and X a connected \(\kappa\)-scheme. For every integer \(i \geqslant 0\), denote by \(\mathrm{X}_i\) the \((i+1)\)-th fibred power of X over \(\kappa\). For every integer \(i \geqslant 0\), the canonical map \[ \pi_0(\mathrm{X}_i) \to \pi_0(\mathrm{X})^{i+1} \] is a bijection. In particular, the simplicial set \(\pi_0(\mathrm{X}_\bullet)\) is contractible.
It suffices to prove the following lemma, which follows by passage to the limit from the Künneth formula. [Detail?]

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.7.2

Let \(\kappa\) be an algebraically closed field and X, Y two connected \(\kappa\)-schemes. Then the fibred product \(\mathrm{X} \times_\kappa \mathrm{Y}\) is connected.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.7.3

Let X be a connected scheme, \(\mathrm{X}' \to \mathrm{X}\) a finite étale morphism and \(\overline{x}\) a geometric point of X localized at a point x. The canonical morphism \(\pi_0(\mathrm{X}'_{\overline{x}}) \to \pi_0(\mathrm{X}')\) is surjective.

Indeed, the image of every connected component of \(\mathrm{X}'\) is an open-and-closed subset of X, hence meets the fibre \(\mathrm{X}'_x\). The morphism \(\pi_0(\mathrm{X}'_x) \to \pi_0(\mathrm{X}')\) is therefore surjective, as is the morphism \(\pi_0(\mathrm{X}'_{\overline{x}}) \to \pi_0(\mathrm{X}'_x)\).
